### Academic backbone (credibility — name-drop the theory)
- **Herold, Heller, Rozemeijer, Mahr (2023), IJPDLM** — SLR of 156 papers via **Dynamic
  Capabilities theory (Teece)**; **nine microfoundations** of Digital Procurement
  Transformation (sensing/seizing/reconfiguring × org/tech/relational). Market check:
  >4,000 digital procurement solutions, €30B+ VC since 2010, only 28% firms at advanced
  adoption (Fraunhofer). **The single strongest citable framework.**
- **Harju, Hallikas, Immonen, Lintukangas (2023), SCM Journal** — 147 Finnish firms, PLS;
  **Information Processing Theory**. Finding: data analytics → *information sharing* (the
  load-bearing mediator) → risk mgmt → resilience. Data analytics alone ≠ resilience.
- **Karttunen, Lintukangas, Hallikas (2023), IJPDLM** — 14 PSM execs; explains WHY firms
  plateau at "digitalized transactions": **data infrastructure + governance** is the primary
  intervention, not tools. Strategic PSM stays analogue. Direct noosphr diagnostic angle.
- **Özkanlisoy & Akkartal (2021), bmij** — 28 firms / 6 industries; concrete applications
  map (e-proc, S&OP, WMS, TMS, RPA, ERP) + named barriers (capex, change-mgmt gaps, silos).
- **Althabatah et al. (2023), Logistics** — SLR 111 papers; Procurement 4.0 tech taxonomy
  (e-proc, blockchain, IoT, big data, AI, additive mfg). Landscape vocabulary.

### Responsible-AI-governance thread (the differentiator vs generic AI consultants)
- **"Public Procurement of AI Systems" (AI & Society, 2024)** — accountability gaps; "many
  hands" problem; scale-of-harm (Michigan MIDAS: 93% false fraud accusations / 40k people);
  procurement-as-policy. Maps to [[Hades]] / LkSG-CSDDD due-diligence.
- **Khan et al. (2024), PLOS Digital Health** — 25-framework review mapped to **PDSA**
  (Plan-Do-Study-Act). Finding: literature is Plan-heavy (84%), Do/Act under-served (52/24%)
  → *position noosphr as the execution + continuous-improvement expert*. LMIC blind-spot = market gap.
- **Stogiannos et al. (2023), Brit. J. Radiology** — 7-pillar AI governance model
  (validation, regs/ethics, audit/QA, R&D, training, PPI, leadership); pre/post-market split.
  Transferable governance checklist for AI-procurement engagements.
- **Brynjolfsson & McAfee, "AI For Real" (HBR 2017)** — AI adoption bottleneck is
  management/imagination, not tech; ML is narrow; supervised learning dominates. Frames
  AI-in-procurement as an *org-change* problem, not a tech play.
- **Accenture "AI and the Future of Procurement" (2017)** — CPO value case: 40% annual
  savings, 30–50% transactional time cut, 50% value-leakage reduction; only 20% deploy AI
  at scale; 4 data categories (supplier/internal/subscription/public).
- **Hallikas "Digitalizing Procurement" (2021)** — dynamic-capability view; digital
  procurement *mediates* analytics → performance (n=101, Finland).

# Distill Candidate: Procurement Market Signals — July 2026

## Synthesis

Two stories from the procurement RSS feed illustrate opposite forces colliding in Q2 2026:

**1. CMA CGM acquires FedEx Supply Chain for $1.4B (expected close 2026)**
Combined entity: ~150 warehouses, ~20K people, 240+ NA locations. Multiyear ocean + air freight agreements follow. Signal: **3PL consolidation accelerating** — fewer independent options for sourcing-channel leverage shifts. The freight vertical is integrating (ocean → warehousing → last-mile), meaning we'll see fewer but bigger players. For procurement teams evaluating logistics vendors, the choice set is shrinking fast.

**1. CMA CGM acquires FedEx Supply Chain for $1.4B (expected close 2026)**
Combined entity: ~150 warehouses, ~20K people, 240+ NA locations. Multiyear ocean + air freight agreements follow. Signal: **3PL consolidation accelerating** — fewer independent options for sourcing-channel leverage shifts. The freight vertical is integrating (ocean → warehousing → last-mile), meaning we'll see fewer but bigger players. For procurement teams evaluating logistics vendors, the choice set is shrinking fast.

**2. McCormick gets $28M tariff refund as Iran war raises costs simultaneously ($3M more expected)**
Supreme Court invalidated country-specific duties; CFO projects ~6% COGS inflation for FY2026 due to tighter freight, higher oil via Strait of Hormuz, elevated energy prices. Classic **procurement-risk scenario: regulatory relief + geopolitical disruption in the same quarter offsetting each other.** The net effect on P&L is ambiguous until you model both forces.

Together these illustrate why procurement teams can't rely on single-signal forecasting anymore — they need multi-factor models that weight regulatory, geopolitical, and consolidation dynamics simultaneously.

And the McCormick case is exactly the kind of scenario where **price benchmarking** becomes essential — internal data tells you what you paid; market benchmarks tell you whether $28M in refunds + Iran-driven freight costs actually improved your position vs competitors.

For Pactum/Tesla prep: clients in logistics/transport verticals will be facing this exact dilemma — fewer 3PL options but simultaneously volatile cost drivers from geopolitical disruption. This is where an AI-powered procurement platform adds value (multi-signal forecasting + benchmarking).

# BATNA and the reservation value

Your **BATNA** — Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement — is what you will do if
this deal falls through. It is not a number you wish for; it is the concrete course of
action you would actually take: renew with the incumbent, switch to the runner-up quote,
run a new RFQ, insource, or do without. The BATNA is the only honest measure of your
power in a negotiation. You are never forced to accept terms worse than your BATNA,
because walking to the alternative is, by definition, better.

## Reservation value = the price of your BATNA

The **reservation value** (or walk-away point) is your BATNA expressed as a deal term:
the worst set of terms at which agreement is still better than walking. Below it, no
deal beats the alternative. Above it, a deal is worth doing. The reservation value is
therefore *derived* — improve your BATNA (line up a credible second supplier, start
earlier so you are not cornered by a deadline) and your reservation value moves in your
favour; let your BATNA decay (single-source, run out of time) and it moves against you.

The **reservation value** (or walk-away point) is your BATNA expressed as a deal term:
the worst set of terms at which agreement is still better than walking. Below it, no
deal beats the alternative. Above it, a deal is worth doing. The reservation value is
therefore *derived* — improve your BATNA (line up a credible second supplier, start
earlier so you are not cornered by a deadline) and your reservation value moves in your
favour; let your BATNA decay (single-source, run out of time) and it moves against you.

Two disciplines follow:

1. **Know your BATNA before you open.** A number you set by feel drifts under pressure.
   A reservation value anchored to a real alternative holds.
2. **Never reveal it.** The reservation point is the one figure that, disclosed, hands
   the counterparty your floor. Argue from interests and criteria, not from "this is the
   lowest I can go."

## How this maps to the engine

## How this maps to the engine

In this system the reservation value is `reservation_utility` — a **hard floor**. The
Boulware concession curve decays from the target toward it but the engine **never
concedes past it**; at the deadline it accepts only an offer that clears the floor, else
it escalates to a human. That is BATNA discipline as code: the agent walks (escalates)
rather than take a deal worse than the alternative.

Note the direction of urgency, which trips people up: **urgency lowers the floor.** If a
deadline makes no-deal worse (a service lapses, a project stalls), your BATNA is weaker,
so you rationally accept a worse deal — the reservation value *drops*. A rule that raised
the floor under urgency would make the agent walk away *more* when it can least afford to.
See [[interests-not-positions]] and [[options-for-mutual-gain]].

# Focus on interests, not positions

A **position** is what a party says they want ("we need a 5% discount"). An **interest**
is *why* they want it (hit a budget number, show a saving to Finance, match a competitor,
de-risk a renewal). Positions collide head-on and produce a win-lose tug of war; interests
often turn out to be compatible, and satisfying them opens moves a positional fight hides.

The move is to ask **why** and **why not** behind every stated position — both yours and
theirs. A supplier holding firm on list price may really be protecting a quarter-end
revenue number; that interest is served just as well by a shorter-term deal, a case study,
or a faster signature — none of which cost you on price. A buyer demanding a lower unit
price may really need predictable total cost; a price cap or a volume tier serves that
interest without the supplier conceding headline rate.

## Why this matters for a procurement agent

Behind most price positions sit interests you can trade against:

Behind most price positions sit interests you can trade against:

- **Revenue timing** → offer an earlier signature or a multi-year commit.
- **Reference / logo** → offer a case study or a referenceable deal.
- **Risk of churn** → offer a longer term for a better rate.
- **Internal budget line** → shift value between one-off and recurring cost.

When you find an interest the other side values highly and you value lightly, you have the
raw material for a trade. That is the bridge to [[options-for-mutual-gain]] and, in this
engine, to **logrolling**: concede on a low-weight term the supplier's interest values,
to hold a high-weight term yours does.

## Guardrail

Interests are inferred, not known. State them as hypotheses to test in the conversation,
never as asserted facts about the counterparty's internal state. In this system the buyer
agent's belief about supplier appetite is exactly that — a *belief* (numbers only), and it
never overrides the deterministic mandate. See [[batna-and-reservation-value]] and
[[objective-criteria]].

# Insist on objective criteria

When two sides argue from will alone — "this is our best price" versus "that's too high" —
the deal goes to whoever is more stubborn, and both sides resent the outcome. **Objective
criteria** replace the contest of wills with a standard neither party controls: a market
benchmark, a published rate card, an independent index, a like-for-like competing quote,
a cost-plus model, precedent from a comparable deal.

The discipline is to agree on the *standard* before arguing the *number*. "What benchmark
should a fair price track?" is a question both sides can reason about; "give me 10% off"
is a demand only one side can win. Once a standard is agreed, the number often follows —
and the party who has to move can do so without losing face, because they are yielding to
a criterion, not to pressure.

## Sources of objective criteria in procurement

## Sources of objective criteria in procurement

- **Market price benchmarks** — third-party rate data, analyst pricing, public tiers.
- **Comparable quotes** — a like-for-like offer from a credible alternative (this also
  *is* your BATNA; see [[batna-and-reservation-value]]).
- **The supplier's own precedent** — prior-year rate, published list less standard
  discount, the rate given to a comparable account.
- **Cost structure** — a should-cost or cost-plus model for a made-to-spec item.
- **Indexation** — tie escalation to a named index (CPI, a commodity index) rather than
  to the supplier's discretion.

## How this maps to the engine

## How this maps to the engine

Objective criteria are what make a `TermSpec`'s `best`/`worst` bounds *defensible* rather
than arbitrary: the target is where a fair benchmark says a good deal sits, the floor is
where the BATNA sits. A counteroffer argued from a benchmark ("comparable renewals cleared
at X") is one the guard can release, because the figure is engine-approved and the
*rationale* is a public standard — not a disclosed internal threshold. Pair with
[[interests-not-positions]] to know which criterion the other side will actually accept.

# Invent options for mutual gain

Most negotiations are treated as fixed-pie: every euro you win, the other side loses. Most
deals are not actually fixed-pie, because the two parties **weight the terms differently.**
When you care a lot about term A and lightly about term B, and the supplier is the reverse,
there is a trade that makes *both* better off — you concede B (cheap to you, valuable to
them) to gain A (valuable to you, cheap to them). Finding those trades is the single
highest-leverage move in a negotiation, and it is invisible if you argue one term at a time.

The practical steps:

The practical steps:

1. **Widen the terms on the table.** Price, payment days, contract length, volume, rebate,
   SLAs, references, ramp schedules, renewal rights. A one-term fight (price) has no room
   for a trade; a multi-term deal has many.
2. **Learn the other side's weights.** Which terms do they defend hardest, and which do
   they give up easily? (This is [[interests-not-positions]] applied to terms.)
3. **Trade across terms, not within one.** Offer movement on a term they value and you
   don't, explicitly *in exchange* for holding a term you value and they don't.
4. **Separate inventing from deciding.** Generate candidate packages first; judge them
   against your target and floor second — don't kill an option before it's fully formed.

## How this maps to the engine — logrolling

## How this maps to the engine — logrolling

This is the core IP of the system. Given a buyer utility `threshold`, the package search
routes concessions to terms the buyer weights lightly and the supplier's belief values
highly — a fractional-knapsack solve that finds the cheapest-to-the-buyer package clearing
the threshold. That *is* "invent options for mutual gain," made deterministic: the engine
never just drops price; it looks for a cross-term trade that holds total buyer utility up.

The visible **give/get** on each counter ("gave: payment 30→45 · held: price €92") is this
principle surfaced — the collaborative trade made legible, so both sides can see the deal
is a package, not a single-number squeeze. Reaching a *collaborative* agreement — the buyer
walking away from a low-weight term to get a high-weight one — is mutual-gain bargaining,
not a concession. See [[batna-and-reservation-value]] for the floor these trades never cross
and [[objective-criteria]] for defending each term's value.

## What actually drives cost

The bill rate you're quoted is **pay rate + agency markup + statutory on-costs**. Suppliers negotiate on the bill rate to keep the split opaque. Your entire job is to **decompose it and negotiate the markup, not the bill rate.** The markup (a percentage or fixed uplift on the worker's pay) is where the agency's margin lives and it is the primary lever. Everything else — placement fees, tenure step-ups, rate-card tiers — is a second cost layer stacked on top.

## The key levers (concrete)

## The key levers (concrete)

- **Force a "rate-card transparency" / pay-rate-plus-markup model.** Demand pay rate, statutory on-costs, and markup shown as separate line items per skill tier. A blended bill rate hides margin; a decomposed one lets you attack the markup alone. This is the single highest-value move.
- **Cap the markup, not the bill rate.** Negotiate the markup percentage down and fix it, so when statutory wages rise, the agency can't ride the increase for extra margin. Markup should be flat per tier, not proportional windfall.
- **Rate cards by skill tier.** Lock a tiered card (admin / skilled / specialist / management) with a defined markup per tier. Stops "tier creep" — agencies re-classifying a worker up a tier to lift the rate.
- **Tenure / equal-treatment step-ups.** In Germany (AÜG), after a defined hire period the agency worker gets equal pay (equal-treatment), and there's a statutory max assignment duration; in the UK the Agency Worker Regulations trigger equal treatment at a set tenure. Suppliers use the *approaching* step-up as a rotation excuse or a rate-hike trigger. Negotiate who bears the step-up cost and pre-agree the conversion path before the tenure clock forces it.
- **Conversion / placement (temp-to-perm) fees.** These are pure margin and highly negotiable. Push for a **sliding scale that decays to zero** the longer the worker was on assignment (they've already earned margin through markup). Never accept a flat headline placement fee.
- **Volume rebates.** Tie a retrospective rebate to committed annual spend or headcount bands. Structure it so it grows with volume and is paid quarterly, not "reviewed annually."
- **Exclusivity vs multi-supplier.** Exclusivity/sole-supply is your biggest bargaining chip — only give it for a hard markup reduction plus a rebate. Otherwise run a **multi-supplier panel** with a lead agency to keep competitive tension.
- **MSP/VMS.** A Managed Service Provider running a Vendor Management System gives spend visibility and consolidated rate cards. Know who funds it: a "supplier-funded" MSP is paid via a levy on agency margin (fine) — but watch for that levy being passed back into your bill rates.

- **Force a "rate-card transparency" / pay-rate-plus-markup model.** Demand pay rate, statutory on-costs, and markup shown as separate line items per skill tier. A blended bill rate hides margin; a decomposed one lets you attack the markup alone. This is the single highest-value move.
- **Cap the markup, not the bill rate.** Negotiate the markup percentage down and fix it, so when statutory wages rise, the agency can't ride the increase for extra margin. Markup should be flat per tier, not proportional windfall.
- **Rate cards by skill tier.** Lock a tiered card (admin / skilled / specialist / management) with a defined markup per tier. Stops "tier creep" — agencies re-classifying a worker up a tier to lift the rate.
- **Tenure / equal-treatment step-ups.** In Germany (AÜG), after a defined hire period the agency worker gets equal pay (equal-treatment), and there's a statutory max assignment duration; in the UK the Agency Worker Regulations trigger equal treatment at a set tenure. Suppliers use the *approaching* step-up as a rotation excuse or a rate-hike trigger. Negotiate who bears the step-up cost and pre-agree the conversion path before the tenure clock forces it.
- **Conversion / placement (temp-to-perm) fees.** These are pure margin and highly negotiable. Push for a **sliding scale that decays to zero** the longer the worker was on assignment (they've already earned margin through markup). Never accept a flat headline placement fee.
- **Volume rebates.** Tie a retrospective rebate to committed annual spend or headcount bands. Structure it so it grows with volume and is paid quarterly, not "reviewed annually."
- **Exclusivity vs multi-supplier.** Exclusivity/sole-supply is your biggest bargaining chip — only give it for a hard markup reduction plus a rebate. Otherwise run a **multi-supplier panel** with a lead agency to keep competitive tension.
- **MSP/VMS.** A Managed Service Provider running a Vendor Management System gives spend visibility and consolidated rate cards. Know who funds it: a "supplier-funded" MSP is paid via a levy on agency margin (fine) — but watch for that levy being passed back into your bill rates.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- **Blended bill rate** that hides the markup — refuse it.
- **Tier creep / mis-classification** to a higher-paid skill band.
- **Statutory-increase pass-through with margin attached** — a minimum-wage rise should move pay rate only, not the markup.
- **Rotating workers out just before the tenure/equal-treatment step-up** to reset the clock and dodge equal pay — bad for continuity and often a false economy.
- **Placement fees framed as non-negotiable** — they are among the most negotiable terms in the category.
- **"All-in" rate that buries overtime, holiday pay, and on-costs** — get the on-cost build-up itemized.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give** longer contract term and committed volume → **get** a lower fixed markup and a bigger rebate. Agencies value guaranteed utilization highly and weight tenor/volume more than you do.
- **Give** exclusivity or panel-lead status → **get** the best markup tier plus a volume rebate.
- **Give** faster payment (shorter payment days) → **get** markup reduction; agencies fund worker payroll weekly, so DSO relief is genuinely valuable to them and cheap for you to concede if cash allows.
- **Get** a decaying conversion-fee schedule and capped markup in exchange for **giving** a multi-year commitment.

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price** (MINIMIZE) = the **markup** on pay rate per skill tier — the real target, not the headline bill rate.
- **payment_days** (MAXIMIZE for the buyer) = a genuine two-way lever here: agencies fund payroll weekly, so *shorter* payment days is something you can concede to buy markup down. Model payment_days as a give-term with high supplier appetite.
- **contract_months** = commitment length; a give-term you trade for markup and rebate.
- **volume** = committed headcount / annual spend that unlocks rebate bands and justifies a lower markup.
- **rebate_pct** (MAXIMIZE) = retrospective volume rebate; a get-term won via exclusivity, term, and volume.
- Conversion/placement fees and tenure step-up cost-bearing are category terms outside the core five — carry them as explicit logrolling give/get terms (low buyer weight, high supplier value on the placement-fee side).

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Decompose the bill rate into pay + on-costs + markup, and negotiate ONLY the markup
- Fix a flat markup per skill tier so statutory wage rises don't hand the agency extra margin
- Lock a tiered rate card to stop tier-creep re-classification into higher bands
- Push conversion/placement fees onto a sliding scale that decays to zero with assignment tenure
- Trade contract length + committed volume for a lower fixed markup and a larger volume rebate
- Concede shorter payment days (agencies fund payroll weekly) to buy the markup down
- Only grant exclusivity/sole-supply for a hard markup cut plus a rebate; else run a multi-supplier panel
- Pre-agree the AÜG/AWR tenure equal-treatment step-up cost and conversion path before the clock forces it

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

No specific market figures are asserted anywhere in this playbook, and none should be. Every number a buyer agent needs must be sourced live at negotiation time: (1) the prevailing market markup range for this category/region/skill tier — benchmark it, do NOT assume a percentage; (2) statutory on-cost rates (employer social contributions, holiday pay, sick pay) for the jurisdiction; (3) the exact tenure thresholds and rules for equal-treatment — Germany's AÜG max assignment duration and equal-pay trigger, and the UK Agency Worker Regulations qualifying period — these are set in law and change, so cite the current statute/regulation with a retrieval date rather than a remembered figure; (4) any MSP levy percentage. IR35 is UK off-payroll tax status and is distinct from AWR equal-treatment — do not conflate them if it enters scope. Confirm which jurisdiction's regime applies before invoking AÜG vs AWR/IR35. A supplier-facing message must never state a benchmark, statutory rate, or tenure threshold that was not retrieved and cited.

## What actually drives cost in Facility Services

FM cost is labour plus a management overlay, not "a service." In cleaning, security, catering, grounds and reception the wage bill is the dominant, largely fixed input; the negotiable margin sits in (a) how the work is *specified*, (b) the *management fee / overhead & profit* on top of pass-through labour, and (c) *risk allocation* (KPIs, indexation, scope creep). If you only push on headline rate, you compress the one part suppliers can defend as "already at cost" and leave the fee, the productivity assumptions and the risk terms untouched. Attack specification and productivity, not just the number.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Output/SLA-based spec, not input/FTE.** Insist the spec pays for a *result* (e.g. defined cleanliness/appearance standard, response times, availability) rather than a headcount roster. Input pricing hands the supplier a guaranteed FTE bill and removes their incentive to be efficient; output pricing transfers productivity risk to them and lets you cut cost through method, not just rate. Open-book on labour + a transparent management fee is the strongest structure for large contracts.
- **Price per m² (and per-service unit) benchmarking.** Normalise every bid to cost per m² per annum (cleaning), per guarding-hour (security), per cover (catering) so you can compare like-for-like and expose loaded rates. Benchmark against your own portfolio and current market data — do not accept a lump sum you can't decompose.
- **KPI / penalty regime with teeth but symmetry.** Tie a meaningful slice of the management fee (fee-at-risk) to a small number of measurable KPIs. Guard against: unmeasurable KPIs, self-reporting, caps that make failure cheap, and "gainshare" that only shares gains. Deductions must be a real remedy, not a token.
- **Bundle vs unbundle deliberately.** Bundling (IFM/TFM: cleaning + security + M&E + catering under one management layer) buys a single fee, one point of accountability and cross-service flex — but adds a margin on subcontracted trades and dampens per-line price tension. Unbundling keeps each line competitive but multiplies management. Use bundling as a *concession you grant for a price/fee cut*, not a default.
- **Scope-creep control.** The classic leak. Nail down a priced schedule of rates (SOR) / rate card for additional and reactive works, define what's in the base fee vs. chargeable, and cap uplift on variations. Without an SOR, every "extra" is sole-sourced to the incumbent at their price.
- **TUPE / staff-transfer.** On outsource or re-tender, transferring staff carry protected terms, pension and often accrued liabilities. Suppliers price a risk premium for unknown workforce data. Neutralise it: provide accurate, warranted TUPE/ELI data early so bidders price the real liability, not a padded guess — this alone can move the number.
- **Self-delivery vs subcontract.** Ask what the prime self-delivers vs subcontracts; subcontracted lines carry a second margin. Push high-volume trades to self-delivery or to your directly-managed subs to strip the layer.
- **Benchmarking & indexation clauses.** Multi-year deals must include a right to benchmark/market-test mid-term and to re-open pricing, plus a *defined* indexation index (a specified labour/wage index, not "supplier's discretion"). Cap indexation and tie any statutory wage rise to evidence.
- **Energy / consumables pass-through.** For energy-touching services (M&E, catering utilities, cleaning consumables) demand pass-through at cost with open invoices — never a margin on energy — and no minimum consumption guarantees.

- **Output/SLA-based spec, not input/FTE.** Insist the spec pays for a *result* (e.g. defined cleanliness/appearance standard, response times, availability) rather than a headcount roster. Input pricing hands the supplier a guaranteed FTE bill and removes their incentive to be efficient; output pricing transfers productivity risk to them and lets you cut cost through method, not just rate. Open-book on labour + a transparent management fee is the strongest structure for large contracts.
- **Price per m² (and per-service unit) benchmarking.** Normalise every bid to cost per m² per annum (cleaning), per guarding-hour (security), per cover (catering) so you can compare like-for-like and expose loaded rates. Benchmark against your own portfolio and current market data — do not accept a lump sum you can't decompose.

Guard against: unmeasurable KPIs, self-reporting, caps that make failure cheap, and "gainshare" that only shares gains. Deductions must be a real remedy, not a token.

- **Scope-creep control.** The classic leak. Nail down a priced schedule of rates (SOR) / rate card for additional and reactive works, define what's in the base fee vs. chargeable, and cap uplift on variations. Without an SOR, every "extra" is sole-sourced to the incumbent at their price.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- Bidding a low mobilisation/year-1 rate, then recovering it through variations and indexation ("buy the contract, win it back on scope").
- FTE/input pricing dressed as "quality assurance" so productivity risk stays with you.
- KPI regimes with fee-at-risk that never bites; self-scored performance.
- Vague TUPE assumptions priced as a premium that never unwinds.
- Bundling everything to obscure line-level margin and lock in incumbency.
- Uncapped, index-linked annual uplifts with no benchmarking right.

## What to trade (give a low-value term to get a high-value one)

## What to trade (give a low-value term to get a high-value one)

- **Give contract length → get lower price/fee.** Longer term lets the supplier amortise mobilisation and TUPE risk; extract a real rate cut and a benchmarking/break clause in return. Never give length without the benchmarking right.
- **Give volume/scope consolidation (bundling) → get management-fee reduction and gainshare.** Their efficiency gain, your fee cut.
- **Give faster payment days → get price or rebate.** Improved cash flow has genuine value to labour-heavy suppliers; trade DPO reduction for a volume rebate or unit-rate cut.
- **Give committed volume → get a rebate/tiered pricing.** Turn spend certainty into a retro rebate rather than a headline discount you can't verify.

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price** = the unit rate (per m² / per hour / per cover) *and* the management fee %. Prioritise fee and productivity assumptions over headline rate.
- **payment_days** = DPO; a low-weight give-away to trade for price or rebate.
- **contract_length** = amortisation lever; concede length only bundled with a benchmarking/market-test right and a break clause.
- **volume** = committed scope/estate; convert to rebate, not just discount.
- **rebate** = the retro/gainshare mechanism where KPI performance, volume commitment and open-book savings are returned to you.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Pay for an output/SLA standard, not FTE headcount — transfer productivity risk to the supplier via open-book labour + transparent management fee
- Decompose every bid to cost per m² / per guarding-hour / per cover and benchmark against portfolio and live market data — reject undecomposable lump sums
- Put a meaningful management-fee-at-risk against 3-5 measurable, independently-verified KPIs; ban self-scoring and toothless caps
- Treat bundling (IFM/TFM) as a concession you grant for a fee cut, not a default — unbundle to keep line-level price tension
- Lock a priced schedule of rates for reactive/additional works and cap variation uplift to kill scope creep
- Provide accurate, warranted TUPE/employee-liability data early so bidders price the real liability instead of a padded risk premium
- Require a mid-term benchmarking / market-test right and a defined, capped indexation index on any multi-year deal
- Force energy and consumables to at-cost pass-through with open invoices and no minimum-consumption guarantee

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

The playbook deliberately contains NO invented figures. Every quantitative claim a buyer would want — subcontract/agency markup %, typical management-fee %, market cost per m² for cleaning, guarding hourly rates, catering cost per cover, prevailing wage/labour indexation rates, and fee-at-risk percentages — must be sourced live at negotiation time ("benchmark the current market markup / rate"), from the buyer's own spend data, a live market benchmark, or the current statutory wage index. A supplier-facing agent must not assert any of these as fact. TUPE / employee-liability specifics (protected terms, pension, accrued liabilities) are real UK/EU transfer-of-undertakings mechanics but the buyer must supply the actual, warranted workforce data for the specific contract rather than let the agent estimate it.

## What drives cost in this category

Agency cost is not one number — it's a stack, and most of it hides below the fee you're quoted. The four cost pools:

1. **Agency labour/fee** — retainer, project fee, FTE cost-plus, or commission. This is the visible line.
2. **Media margin** — where the real money leaks. On principal/proprietary media the agency buys inventory itself and resells it to you at an opaque markup; on "gross" buying you pay rate-card and the agency keeps the difference. **AVBs/rebates** (agency volume bonuses paid by media owners) may never reach you unless the contract says they must.
3. **Rights & usage** — talent, music, and asset licences that expire. Under-scoped buyouts create forced re-negotiations later at zero leverage.
4. **Scope creep** — out-of-SOW work billed at ad-hoc rates, and "included" hours that quietly inflate the retainer.

The category's defining trap: the supplier steers you to negotiate the fee (small, visible) while protecting media margin and rebates (large, invisible).

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Decouple media from creative.** Separate SOWs/contracts for media buying vs creative removes the bundle that lets an agency subsidise a cheap fee with hidden media margin. Insist on a media-neutral, auditable buying arrangement.
- **Media transparency clause.** Contract for **net (disclosed) buying**, full pass-through of all AVBs/rebates/free space, principal-media disclosure, and an **independent audit right** with a defined look-back window. No audit right = no transparency.
- **Fee-model choice is a lever, not a given.** Retainer (predictable, prone to over-staffing), project/SOW (disciplined, admin-heavy), FTE cost-plus (transparent labour, cap the multiplier), or performance/incentive (aligns interest, needs clean KPIs). Push cost-plus + capped margin where you can see the inputs; use performance to claw back fixed fee.
- **Rate card + blended rate.** Attack the seniority mix, not just hourly rates — agencies pad plans with senior time. Negotiate a **blended rate** tied to an agreed pyramid, cap principal/EVP hours, and require timesheet substantiation.
- **SOW discipline.** Every engagement gets a written scope, deliverables, and a change-control mechanism. Kill "T&M for anything not listed."
- **Rights/usage buyouts.** Negotiate usage term, territory, and renewal cost **up front** in the original SOW — never after the asset is live.
- **Pitch leverage.** Competitive pitch or credible incumbent-review threat is your single biggest price mover; roster consolidation (volume) is the second.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- Cheap headline fee, recovered through media margin and unshared rebates.
- "Blended rate" quoted on a senior-heavy pyramid you never agreed.
- Retainer hours "used up" early to justify out-of-scope billing.
- Narrow usage rights that force a paid renewal when the campaign performs.
- Rebate/AVB language that's silent or defers to "market practice" — meaning the agency keeps it.

## What to trade (give low-value to get high-value)

## What to trade (give low-value to get high-value)

- **Give longer contract length; get fee reduction + locked rate card + guaranteed rebate pass-through.** Agencies value tenure certainty highly; you weight fee and transparency higher.
- **Give volume/roster consolidation; get blended-rate cut and better usage terms.** Consolidated spend is cheap for you to promise and highly valued by them.
- **Give faster payment days; get margin/fee concession.** Cash flow is often the supplier's top ask — trade a term you weight lightly for one you weight heavily.
- **Hold the line on transparency and audit rights** — these are near-zero cost to you and directly attack the hidden cost pool. Never trade them away for a headline discount.

## Mapping to the engine terms

## Mapping to the engine terms

- **price** → agency fee level, blended rate, and disclosed media margin/markup.
- **payment_days** → billing terms; a cheap give for you, a strong get for the agency (cash flow).
- **contract_months** → tenure; trade length for fee and transparency commitments.
- **volume_units** → consolidated/committed spend or roster share; concede to unlock rate cuts.
- **rebate_pct** → the AVB/rebate pass-through and any performance/incentive claw-back — the category's highest-leverage hidden term. Weight it heavily; it is where category cost actually hides.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Decouple media from creative into separate SOWs so a cheap fee can't be subsidised by hidden media margin
- Contract for net/disclosed media buying with 100% AVB/rebate pass-through and an independent audit right + look-back window
- Choose the fee model as a lever: prefer cost-plus with a capped multiplier or performance-linked over open-ended retainers
- Negotiate the blended rate against an agreed seniority pyramid and cap principal/EVP hours, not just headline hourly rates
- Enforce SOW discipline with written scope + change control; eliminate open T&M for unlisted work
- Lock rights/usage term, territory, and renewal cost up front in the original SOW, never after the asset is live
- Use a competitive pitch or credible incumbent review as the primary price mover; consolidate the roster for volume leverage
- Trade contract length and faster payment for fee cuts, locked rate cards, and guaranteed rebate pass-through

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook deliberately states NO specific numbers. Any figure a real negotiation needs must be sourced live and never asserted by the agent: (1) typical agency markup / media margin percentages — benchmark against the current market and the specific agency's disclosed inputs; (2) prevailing AVB/rebate levels by market and media type — these vary by country, media owner, and regulatory regime and must be pulled from current data or an audit, not assumed; (3) blended-rate and hourly-rate benchmarks by role/seniority and geography — source from a current rate-card benchmark; (4) standard payment-term norms and typical retainer sizing for the client's region/sector. The agent must present these as "benchmark this live" rather than fabricate. Also: the existence and enforceability of rebate pass-through and audit rights is jurisdiction-dependent (e.g. differing disclosure norms across markets) — verify against the governing law and any applicable industry transparency guidance before asserting a contractual entitlement.

## What drives cost

Freight cost is **linehaul rate + fuel + accessorials + surcharge creep**, and the total is dominated by things buyers forget to fix in the contract. The linehaul (the base rate per lane/mode) is what everyone negotiates; the money leaks through fuel surcharge (BAF/FSC), accessorials, and detention/demurrage — the "below-the-line" items where carriers recover margin they conceded on the headline rate. Cost also swings with **mode** (within road, FTL is cheapest per unit, then LTL, then parcel; for international long-haul, ocean is far cheaper per kg than air — the two axes are independent, not a single ascending line), **lane balance** (backhaul/headhaul imbalance — a carrier's empty return leg is where you win), and **commitment vs. spot** (committed volume buys stability; spot buys flexibility, and each is cheaper than the other at different times of the cycle). Award is usually run as a **tender/RFP** across a lane matrix, which is your single biggest lever: you're not negotiating one number, you're allocating a portfolio.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Decompose the price.** Never accept an all-in rate. Split linehaul, fuel, and accessorials so each is negotiated and indexed separately. An all-in rate hides where the margin sits.
- **Cap and formula-fix the fuel surcharge (BAF/FSC).** Tie it to a published index (e.g., a national diesel/bunker index), fix the peg point and the escalation slope, and **cap it**. An uncapped, carrier-defined FSC table is the classic trap — the carrier sets both the trigger and the step size.
- **Attack accessorials line by line.** Detention, demurrage, liftgate, residential, reweigh, layover, redelivery, waiting time. Negotiate free time (detention/demurrage), per-unit rates, and caps. These are pure recovery items.
- **Index-linked pricing over flat annual rates.** Agree the rate = base + published index delta, with a symmetric collar (moves down as well as up). This kills mid-term "market has moved" reopeners.
- **Volume commitment as currency, not a gift.** Commit volume to buy rate — but make the minimum-volume clause soft (band, not a hard floor with penalties) so a demand dip doesn't trigger a shortfall charge.
- **Incoterms decide who pays and who bears risk.** Moving from EXW/FCA toward DAP/DDP shifts cost and risk to the seller but usually raises the unit price; moving toward FCA/FOB lets you control the freight leg and tender it yourself. Choose deliberately — it's a cost-allocation lever, not a formality.
- **Award allocation in the tender.** Split award across carriers (primary/secondary) to keep competitive tension; use the RFP to make carriers bid against their own backhaul lanes.

## Category traps suppliers use

## Category traps suppliers use

- **Uncapped/self-defined fuel tables** that ratchet the effective rate above the negotiated linehaul.
- **Accessorial ambiguity** — undefined detention free time, "market" demurrage, discretionary reweigh/reclass on LTL.
- **GRI / peak-season surcharges** dropped in mid-term when not contractually excluded or capped.
- **Minimum-volume penalties** framed as your commitment but structured so any shortfall bills at a punitive rate.
- **Spot-rate bait** — a low committed rate that relies on you tendering overflow to their expensive spot desk.

## What to trade (give / get)

Map to the engine terms — **price, payment days, contract length, volume, rebate**:

Map to the engine terms — **price, payment days, contract length, volume, rebate**:

- **Give contract length, get rate.** A longer term (with index-linked pricing and a cap) is low-cost to you and lets the carrier plan capacity — trade it for a lower linehaul and a firmer FSC cap.
- **Give volume commitment, get rate + a rebate tier.** Route committed volume to the carrier's strong (backhaul) lanes — high value to them, and you'd move it anyway.
- **Give payment days, get price.** Faster payment (fewer payment days) is real cash-flow value to an asset-heavy carrier; concede it to pull down the linehaul or accessorial caps. This is a low-weight term for many buyers and high-value to the supplier — a clean logroll.
- **Take rebate over headline discount** when the carrier resists a rate cut: a volume-triggered rebate protects them on paper while returning the same money to you, and only pays out if the volume materializes.
- **Hold the line on FSC cap and accessorials** — these are your high-weight terms; don't spend concessions there.

## Engine mapping

## Engine mapping

Price = decomposed linehaul + capped FSC (minimize). Contract length and volume are the currency you concede to move price. Payment days are a low-weight give with high supplier value. Rebate is the face-saving substitute for a headline rate cut, tied to committed volume.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Decompose the all-in rate into linehaul + fuel + accessorials and negotiate/index each separately
- Cap the fuel surcharge and peg it to a published diesel/bunker index with a fixed formula, never a carrier-defined table
- Negotiate every accessorial line by line — detention/demurrage free time, per-unit rates, and hard caps
- Use index-linked pricing with a symmetric collar to block mid-term 'market moved' reopeners
- Trade longer contract length (with a rate cap) for a lower linehaul and firmer FSC cap
- Route committed volume to the carrier's backhaul/strong lanes to win rate for volume you'd ship anyway
- Concede faster payment days (high value to asset-heavy carriers) to pull down price and accessorial caps
- Take a volume-triggered rebate when the carrier won't cut the headline rate, and split tender award primary/secondary to keep tension

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook deliberately contains NO specific numbers — no benchmark rates, no typical FSC caps, no detention free-time hours, no markup percentages, no rebate thresholds. Every such figure must be sourced live before use: benchmark the current linehaul rate per lane/mode against a live rate index or recent bids; look up the current published diesel/bunker index value and its historical peg for the FSC formula; pull current market detention/demurrage free-time norms and per-hour/per-day rates for the relevant mode and region; and confirm current peak-season/GRI surcharge levels from the carrier's published tariff. A supplier-facing agent must not assert any of these as fact — state them as "to be benchmarked" and cite the live source and retrieval date.

## What drives cost

HR services vendors price on a **PEPM (per-employee-per-month) × headcount × months** base, plus one-time **implementation/setup**, plus per-module and per-transaction add-ons. Because PEPM multiplies across your whole workforce and the full contract term, a small unit concession is a large absolute win — PEPM is the term that actually moves total contract value. Suppliers know this, so they defend PEPM hardest and give ground on things that look generous but cost them little (implementation discounts, a free module trial, "waived" fees that reappear at renewal).

The second cost driver is **switching friction**: once your payroll/HRIS is live, migrating off is painful, so vendors quote thin on year 1 and recover margin at renewal and through uplift clauses. Your leverage is highest **before signature** and at **renewal with a credible alternative** — nowhere else.

## The key levers (concrete)

## The key levers (concrete)

- **Unbundle then rebundle.** Force a line-item quote: core payroll, HRIS, benefits admin, time & attendance, reporting, support tier, each priced separately. Vendors bundle to hide which modules carry margin. Once unbundled, drop modules you don't use and rebundle only what you need — the bundle discount should survive, not the padding.
- **Split implementation from recurring and cap it.** Implementation is a one-time sunk cost the vendor wants amortized into PEPM. Negotiate it as a **fixed, milestone-based fee with acceptance criteria**, and tie final payment to go-live sign-off. Never accept "implementation billed at time & materials."
- **Kill data-migration as a profit center.** Migrating your historical employee/payroll data should be **in-scope of implementation, not a surprise line item**. Demand it be quoted upfront and fixed.
- **Benefits-admin carrier economics.** If the vendor also brokers or administers benefits, ask directly whether they receive **carrier commissions, overrides, or bonuses**. That's a hidden revenue stream — use it to press the admin PEPM down, or require fee transparency / commission offset.
- **Price protection, not just price.** Win a **capped annual uplift** (or index-linked cap) for the whole term. An open renewal clause lets the vendor claw back every year-1 concession.
- **Offboarding and data portability as a signed term.** Get contractual **data export in a standard format at no incremental fee, plus a transition-assistance period** at pre-agreed rates. Without it, the vendor owns your exit and prices accordingly at renewal.
- **SLA on payroll accuracy with teeth.** A payroll-accuracy / on-time-filing SLA with **service credits** is standard and cheap to ask for; a vendor refusing credits is signaling low confidence.

## Category-specific traps

## Category-specific traps

- **"Free implementation" financed through a longer lock.** The setup discount is recovered via a multi-year commit and renewal uplift. Price the whole term, not year 1.
- **PEPM that only applies at a headcount tier you'll dip below.** Confirm the rate holds across your realistic headcount range; get a **true-down**, not just a true-up.
- **Per-transaction fees** (off-cycle runs, W-2/year-end, garnishments, corrections) buried in an appendix. Get the full fee schedule and cap or waive high-frequency ones.
- **Module bundling where one sticky module subsidizes the rest** — you can't drop it later without repricing everything.

## What to trade (give to get)

Give the vendor the things they value that cost you little; take PEPM and exit protection.

Give the vendor the things they value that cost you little; take PEPM and exit protection.

- **Give a longer term / commitment; get lower PEPM + a firm uplift cap + a mid-term exit-for-cause.** Length is your cheapest currency — but only trade it for locked economics, never a bare discount.
- **Give faster payment (shorter payment days); get PEPM or implementation reduction.** Improved cash timing is real value to the vendor.
- **Give committed/growing headcount (volume); get a lower PEPM tier and a rebate on overage.**
- **Give a reference/case-study or a phased rollout; get waived migration and capped implementation.**

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price →** PEPM (primary) and the fixed implementation fee (secondary). This is the high-weight term for the buyer.
- **payment days →** invoicing terms; concede faster payment to buy PEPM down.
- **contract length →** the multi-year lock. High supplier value, low buyer cost *if* paired with an uplift cap and an exit clause — the classic logrolling give.
- **volume →** committed headcount / seats; drives the PEPM tier.
- **rebate →** growth rebate or overage credit as headcount scales, and service-credit SLA on payroll accuracy.

Trade length, payment days, and volume (low buyer weight, high supplier value) to protect PEPM and offboarding rights (high buyer weight).

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Force a line-item PEPM + per-module + per-transaction quote before discussing any bundle discount
- Cap implementation as a fixed milestone fee tied to go-live acceptance; never T&M
- Fold data migration into implementation scope at a fixed price — refuse it as a separate line item
- Demand disclosure of benefits-carrier commissions/overrides and use them to press admin PEPM down
- Win a capped annual uplift for the full term so year-1 concessions can't be clawed back at renewal
- Sign offboarding data-export in standard format at no fee, plus a priced transition-assistance period
- Attach a payroll-accuracy / on-time-filing SLA with service credits
- Trade contract length, faster payment, and committed headcount to buy PEPM down and lock exit rights

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook deliberately contains NO benchmark numbers — no market PEPM range, no "typical" implementation-to-recurring ratio, no standard uplift-cap percentage, no carrier-commission rate, no SLA-credit magnitude. Every one of those would need to be sourced live for the specific market, headcount band, and vendor before a buyer agent asserts it. Any place the agent wants to say "the market rate is X" or "vendors typically mark up Y%," it must first pull a current benchmark (recent comparable quotes, an advisory/benchmarking source, or an RFP field) and cite it with a retrieval date — it must never state a figure from memory to the supplier. Whether a given vendor actually earns carrier commissions is also vendor-specific and must be confirmed by direct disclosure, not assumed.

## What drives cost

Financial and insurance cost is not "a price" — it is **risk transfer plus intermediation plus the market cycle**, and each has a different owner you can play. For **insurance**, the premium is fundamentally driven by your own loss ratio (claims paid ÷ premium earned over the look-back period), your exposure base (revenue, headcount, payroll, insured values, fleet size), and where in the **hard/soft market cycle** you renew. In a soft market capacity is abundant and insurers compete on price; in a hard market they push rate, cut limits and widen exclusions. You cannot control the cycle, but you can time renewals, restructure retention, and re-market to exploit it. For **banking and treasury**, cost hides in the spread and the fee stack — FX spread over mid-market, transaction/payment fees, facility/commitment fees on undrawn credit, cash-management and custody pricing — not in the headline rate a relationship manager quotes.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Insurance premium: attack the loss ratio narrative.** Present a clean, well-documented claims history and risk-improvement actions (loss control, safety, cyber controls) so the underwriter reprices your actual risk, not a portfolio average. A good story on frequency/severity trend is worth real rate.
- **Move up the retention.** Higher deductible / excess / self-insured retention (SIR) transfers predictable small claims back to you and cuts premium — you stop paying an insurer's margin to swap dollars. Model the retained-loss expected value against the premium saving; only retain what your balance sheet can absorb.
- **Broker: commission vs. fee, and full disclosure.** Demand the broker's remuneration in writing — commission (a % of premium the insurer pays them, which rises with your premium and misaligns incentives) vs. a fixed fee for defined scope. A flat fee aligns them to lower your premium. Ask about contingent/profit commissions and market-derived income too.
- **Multi-line / multi-year structuring.** Bundling lines (property, liability, cyber, D&O) or locking a multi-year deal buys premium stability and can earn a discount — but only trade it for a rate concession, a rate-cap, and a mid-term re-marketing/exit right. Never hand multi-year certainty away for free.
- **Banking: unbundle and benchmark the spread.** Separate FX spread, per-transaction fees, and facility/commitment fees; benchmark FX against mid-market and negotiate a fixed spread, not "market." Compete the wallet — banks cross-subsidise lending with fee income, so use credit as leverage on cash-management pricing.
- **Captive / alternative risk transfer.** For large, stable programmes, a captive or protected cell retains underwriting profit and smooths cycle volatility. It's a strategic structure, not a line-item — flag it for treasury/finance to evaluate.
- **Re-market to create tension.** The single biggest insurance lever is a credible broker-run market submission to multiple carriers. Incumbent renewal quotes soften fast when a competing capacity is visibly at the table.

## Category-specific traps

## Category-specific traps

- **Commission opacity** — broker paid a % of the premium they're meant to reduce; contingent commissions never disclosed.
- **Silent hardening** — renewal holds the premium but quietly cuts limits, raises the retention, or adds exclusions. Compare terms, not just price.
- **Rate vs. premium sleight-of-hand** — a "flat rate" on a growing exposure base still raises your premium.
- **FX "no fee" that hides the spread** — the cost moved into a wider spread over mid-market.
- **Undrawn-facility and commitment fees** priced as boilerplate; auto-renew and profit-share clauses that outlive their value.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give contract length (multi-year), get a rate cut + rate cap.** Multi-year is low-cost to you if you keep a re-marketing/exit right; it's high-value stability to the insurer. Trade it — never gift it.
- **Give retention (higher deductible/SIR), get lower premium.** You concede a "term" you can absorb to pull down the price you weight most.
- **Give volume/consolidation (multi-line, whole wallet to one bank), get fee/spread reduction or a rebate.** Certainty of the whole programme is the currency.
- **Give faster payment / annual-in-advance premium, get price.** Cash timing has genuine value to a carrier or bank — a clean logroll.
- **Take a rebate/profit-share when the counterparty won't cut the headline** — a low-loss-ratio profit-share or volume rebate returns the money only if performance holds.

## Engine mapping

## Engine mapping

- **price** = premium / effective rate, broker fee, FX spread, and the fee stack — minimize; prioritise structure over headline.
- **payment_days** = premium payment timing / DPO — a low-weight give to trade for price.
- **contract_length** = multi-year lock — concede only bundled with a rate cap and re-marketing/exit right.
- **volume** = exposure base, multi-line bundle, banking wallet — convert to rebate, not just discount.
- **rebate** = profit-share / low-loss-ratio return / volume rebate — the face-saving substitute for a rate cut.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Reprice on your actual loss ratio with a documented claims history and risk-improvement story, not a portfolio average
- Raise deductible / excess / self-insured retention to transfer predictable small claims back and cut premium — sized to your balance sheet
- Force broker remuneration into the open: fixed fee over premium-based commission, and disclose contingent/profit commissions
- Run a credible multi-carrier re-marketing submission to create capacity tension against the incumbent renewal
- Unbundle banking cost — negotiate FX as a fixed spread over mid-market, and attack transaction, facility and commitment fees separately
- Trade multi-year / multi-line lock only for a rate cut, a rate cap, and a mid-term re-marketing / exit right
- Time the renewal and restructure retention to the hard/soft market cycle; compare terms (limits, exclusions) not just premium
- Flag captive / protected-cell / ART structures for large stable programmes to retain underwriting profit and smooth the cycle

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook contains NO invented figures, and a supplier-facing agent must assert none. Every quantitative input a buyer needs must be sourced live at negotiation time: (1) the client's own loss ratio, claims frequency/severity and exposure base — from the buyer's actual loss runs and financials, never estimated; (2) typical or "market" broker commission percentages and whether contingent/profit commissions apply — obtain the specific broker's disclosed remuneration, do not quote a rule-of-thumb %; (3) prevailing insurance rate movement (hard vs. soft market direction and magnitude by line) — from a live market/renewal-rate index at renewal date; (4) current mid-market FX rates and a fair spread benchmark — from a live rate feed, not memory; (5) benchmark banking transaction, facility/commitment and cash-management fees — from a live market benchmark or the buyer's competing bids; (6) deductible/SIR premium-saving trade-offs — must be modelled on the buyer's actual claims distribution, not a generic ratio; (7) captive feasibility thresholds — require actuarial/tax analysis, not a stated minimum premium figure. On EU/German context: any regulatory or disclosure point (e.g. insurance distribution remuneration transparency, banking fee-disclosure duties) is a real obligation area but a specific statutory rate, threshold, or citation must be sourced live with its source and retrieval date before it is stated as fact — an unsourced compliance figure is not a finding.

## What actually drives cost

List price is theatre. In IT hardware the real cost stack is: OEM cost basis (component/BOM, moves with memory/panel/CPU cycles), the reseller/VAR margin layered on top, plus attach services (warranty, imaging, deployment, disposal) where the fat margin hides. You are almost never negotiating with the OEM — you're negotiating with a reseller/VAR who buys under an OEM "deal reg" or "special bid." That means two prices exist: street margin on hardware (thin, commoditised) and services/attach margin (thick, opaque). Push discount off hardware and suppliers shrug; push on attach and standardisation and you move real money. In the EU add: VAT handling, WEEE/take-back obligations, and Ecodesign/energy-label positioning that suppliers use to justify premium SKUs.

## The key levers (concrete)

## The key levers (concrete)

- **Volume + standardisation together.** Cutting your SKU count (one laptop line, one monitor, one dock) is worth more than raw volume — it lets the VAR forecast, hold stock, and pull a bigger OEM special bid. Trade a signed SKU-reduction commitment for a deeper standing discount.
- **Buy vs lease/DaaS.** Lease/Device-as-a-Service converts capex to a monthly rate but bundles finance cost, residual-value assumptions, and end-of-term risk (fair-wear clauses, missing-device penalties). Always demand the buy-equivalent price to expose the embedded finance/residual margin. DaaS is a lever, not a gift.
- **Refresh cycle.** Extending 3→4 years cuts annual spend but raises failure/warranty exposure. Use the refresh cadence as a bargaining chip: a committed refresh volume over a fixed term is exactly what earns tiered pricing.
- **Trade-in / asset buyback.** Old fleet has residual value. Force it to be quoted as a *separate line credit*, not baked into a "net" price where you can't see it.
- **Extended warranty vs self-insure.** For large fleets, self-insuring the tail (buying spares, break-fix pool) often beats blanket 4th/5th-year warranty. Price the warranty attach separately and challenge it.
- **Bundling hardware + support.** Suppliers push bundles to hide attach margin. Unbundle to price each, then re-bundle only if the bundle genuinely beats the sum.
- **End-of-life / last-time-buy.** When a model goes EOL, negotiate a last-time-buy at protected pricing plus spares — don't get forced onto a pricier successor mid-cycle.
- **Freight, imaging/staging.** Zero-touch deployment, asset tagging, BIOS config are real services but heavily marked up. Cap or waive freight on volume; competitively benchmark staging per-unit.
- **Price protection.** Component costs swing both ways. Get a clause that passes *decreases* through, not just protects the supplier's *increases* — most one-sided clauses only protect the seller.

- **Volume + standardisation together.** Cutting your SKU count (one laptop line, one monitor, one dock) is worth more than raw volume — it lets the VAR forecast, hold stock, and pull a bigger OEM special bid. Trade a signed SKU-reduction commitment for a deeper standing discount.

## Category traps suppliers use

## Category traps suppliers use

- "Net" pricing that hides the trade-in credit and the attach margin.
- Bundled DaaS quotes with no buy-equivalent, hiding finance + residual margin.
- One-directional price-protection (increases pass through, decreases don't).
- Free imaging/freight "included" — it was priced into the unit.
- Fair-wear-and-tear and end-of-lease device-loss penalties that claw back the lease saving.
- EOL forcing onto a costlier successor without a last-time-buy option.

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give payment days** (offer faster pay / shorter terms) **→ get price** or a deeper rebate tier. Cash timing is cheap for a well-funded buyer and valuable to a VAR carrying inventory.
- **Give contract length + committed volume** (multi-year, standardised SKUs) **→ get lower unit price and stronger price protection.**
- **Give forecast accuracy / SKU reduction → get rebate.** Predictability is what unlocks the OEM special bid behind the reseller.
- **Take rebate over headline discount** when spend is uncertain — it self-adjusts to actual volume and is auditable at year-end.

## Mapping to the engine terms

## Mapping to the engine terms

- **price** — hardware unit price after special-bid/deal-reg; keep attach/services on separate lines so this stays clean.
- **payment_days** — your cheap give; concede here to buy price/rebate.
- **contract_length** — multi-year term = the commitment that earns tiered pricing and price protection.
- **volume** — committed units *plus* SKU standardisation; both feed the discount tier.
- **rebate** — back-end volume/growth rebate; prefer when forward volume is uncertain, and use it to bank trade-in credits and overachievement.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Trade a signed SKU-standardisation commitment for a deeper standing discount — fewer SKUs let the VAR forecast and pull a bigger OEM special bid
- Always demand the buy-equivalent price on any lease/DaaS quote to expose embedded finance and residual-value margin
- Force trade-in / buyback to appear as a separate line credit, never buried in a 'net' price

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

Every quantitative figure a buyer agent would want here MUST be sourced live at negotiation time — none are asserted in this playbook because I will not invent them. Specifically: (1) typical/achievable discount-off-list percentages for any OEM or category — these depend on the live OEM deal-reg/special-bid and current channel margin, get them from actual quotes/benchmarks. (2) VAR/reseller margin percentages on hardware vs. attach/services — real but never assume a number; derive from the unbundled quote. (3) Residual-value and finance-rate assumptions inside any lease/DaaS quote — request the supplier's actual figures and the buy-equivalent. (4) Trade-in/buyback residual values — must come from a current asset appraisal, not a rule of thumb. (5) Extended-warranty attach cost and fleet failure rates used to justify self-insuring — pull from the supplier quote and your own fleet history. (6) EU statutory items: current standard/reduced VAT rate for the relevant member state, WEEE/take-back cost obligations, and Ecodesign/energy-label thresholds — verify against the live statute/directive with a retrieval date; do not state a rate from memory. (7) Any 'market average' payment-term (e.g. net-30/45/60) benchmark — confirm against current category data. A supplier-facing agent must assert none of these as fact; if a number is needed it must be quoted from a sourced document or the supplier's own submission.

## What drives cost

R&M cost is **labour rate + parts markup + the mix of planned vs reactive work**, wrapped in an SLA. The headline everyone stares at is the hourly labour rate, but the real money sits in three places buyers forget to fix: (a) the **PPM/reactive split** — how much you pre-buy as a fixed planned-maintenance contract vs pay per callout, (b) the **parts markup** applied on top of pass-through spares, and (c) the **SLA regime** — whether response/fix times carry real penalties or are self-reported. A pure day-rate contract with an open "parts at cost plus markup" line and soft SLAs is the supplier's dream: they recover on parts and callouts whatever you squeeze on the rate. Attack the *structure* — decompose labour from parts, fix the PPM scope, and put teeth on the SLA — not just the number.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Decompose labour rate and parts markup separately.** Never accept an all-in "per visit" or "per repair" price. Split the fully-loaded labour rate (and its bands: normal / out-of-hours / emergency / travel) from the parts markup on pass-through spares. Both must be named and negotiated on their own line; an all-in figure hides where margin lives.
- **PPM as fixed-price, reactive as a capped rate card.** Buy planned preventive maintenance as a fixed annual price for a defined asset list and task schedule; price reactive/callout work against a pre-agreed **schedule of rates** with call-out fees, response bands, and a cap on markup for variations. Without a rate card, every breakdown is sole-sourced to the incumbent at their price.
- **First-time-fix rate as a headline KPI.** A high first-time-fix rate collapses repeat visits, travel, and downtime — it's often worth more than the labour rate itself. Make it a measured, penalised KPI, not a marketing claim.
- **Response/fix-time SLAs with fee-at-risk.** Separate *response* time from *fix* (restore) time; put a meaningful slice of the contract value at risk against a small number of measurable SLAs, with independent measurement (CMMS logs, not supplier self-scoring) and deductions that are a real remedy, not a token cap.
- **Fixed-price over T&M where the scope is knowable.** T&M rewards slow, parts-heavy work; fixed-price or a priced task catalogue transfers efficiency risk to the supplier. Reserve T&M for genuinely unpredictable works, and cap it.
- **Multi-asset / portfolio consolidation.** Bundling estate-wide or multi-site assets under one contract buys density and one accountability point — treat it as a concession you grant for a rate/markup cut, not a default that dampens line-level tension.
- **Spares stocking, obsolescence, and parts ownership.** Fix who owns and funds consignment/critical spares, agree consumption transparency (open invoices, no margin games on stock), and get an obsolescence-management commitment (notice, last-time-buy, alternatives) so end-of-life parts don't become a captive-pricing event.
- **Self-perform vs subcontract.** Ask what the prime self-delivers vs subcontracts; subcontracted trades carry a second margin. Push high-volume trades to self-delivery or your directly-managed subs to strip the layer.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- Low labour rate bid, margin recovered on **parts markup** and callout fees ("buy the rate, win it back on parts").
- **T&M creep** — jobs that could be fixed-price billed by the hour with generous travel and diagnosis time.
- **SLA theatre** — self-reported response times, unmeasurable "fix" definitions, deduction caps that make failure cheap.
- **Callout-fee stacking** — a call-out charge plus minimum-hours plus travel on every reactive visit.
- **Obsolescence as a pricing event** — captive markups on end-of-life spares with no last-time-buy or alternative.
- **PPM padding** — over-frequent planned visits billed as prevention, low first-time-fix so reactive volume stays high.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give contract length → get lower labour rate + firmer markup cap.** A longer term lets the supplier amortise mobilisation, training, and spares investment — extract a real rate cut, a capped parts markup, and a mid-term benchmarking/break right in return. Never give length without the benchmarking clause.
- **Give portfolio/volume consolidation → get rate cut + a rebate tier.** Estate density is high-value to them; convert spend certainty into a retro rebate, not just a headline discount you can't verify.
- **Give faster payment days → get price or markup concession.** Faster payment is genuine cash-flow value to labour-and-parts-heavy suppliers; it's a low-weight give for many buyers — a clean logroll for a lower rate or tighter caps.
- **Take rebate over a headline cut** when the supplier resists on rate — a volume- or SLA-triggered rebate protects them on paper and only pays out if the commitment materialises.
- **Hold the line on the SLA penalty regime, parts markup cap, and first-time-fix KPI** — these are your high-weight terms; don't spend concessions there.

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price** = the fully-loaded labour rate *and* the parts markup % *and* the PPM fixed price / callout rate card. Prioritise markup cap and PPM/reactive structure over headline hourly rate.

- **contract_length** = amortisation lever; concede length only bundled with a benchmarking/market-test right and a break clause.

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook contains NO invented figures, and a supplier-facing agent must not assert any of the following as fact — each must be sourced live at negotiation time (from the buyer's own spend/CMMS data, a live market benchmark, or the supplier's warranted data) and cited with a retrieval date: prevailing fully-loaded labour rates and the uplift for out-of-hours / emergency / travel bands (by trade and EU/German region); "typical" or acceptable parts markup percentages on pass-through spares; call-out fee levels and minimum-billable-hours norms; a benchmark or "good" first-time-fix rate; PPM visit frequencies and the fixed annual PPM price for a given asset list; SLA response/fix-time targets and the fee-at-risk / deduction percentages that count as meaningful; rebate thresholds and tiers; and last-time-buy / obsolescence lead times. Any statutory or index figure — the relevant labour/wage indexation index used for multi-year escalation, and any EU/German legal points (e.g. warranty/liability periods, or LkSG/CSDDD supply-chain due-diligence obligations if in scope) — must be pulled from the current official source, not recalled from memory. The actual asset register, spares criticality list, and warranted labour data for the specific contract must come from the buyer/supplier, never be estimated by the agent.

## What actually drives cost

You are buying professional judgment and liability-bearing hours, not a product. Cost is driven by (a) the **fee basis** — percentage-of-construction-cost, lump-sum, or time-charge — (b) the **defined scope and deliverable set**, (c) the **risk the firm carries** (indemnity levels, liability caps, IP), and (d) **change-order leakage**. The single biggest lever is fee basis. Percentage-of-cost fees perversely reward the designer when the build gets more expensive — the incentive is backwards. Lump-sum on a *frozen, fully-defined scope* aligns the firm to your budget; time-charge with a cap suits genuinely undefined early work. Attack the structure before the rate.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Fee basis conversion.** Push percentage-of-construction-cost toward lump-sum once scope is defined. If a percentage stays (common under HOAI-style stage fee logic in Germany), fix the *construction cost basis* early and cap it, so scope growth doesn't auto-inflate the fee. HOAI fee tables are now advisory, not binding minimums, since the 2019 ECJ ruling and the 2021 reform — fees are freely negotiable. Do not accept "HOAI mandates this rate" as fact.
- **Stage/phase gating (RIBA Plan of Work / HOAI Leistungsphasen).** Tie fee to defined work stages (RIBA 0–7 / HOAI LP 1–9) with a go/no-go and a payment release at each gate. This lets you exit after concept without buying detailed design, and stops front-loaded billing.
- **Scope and deliverable specification.** The costliest ambiguity is what's *in* the base fee. Enumerate deliverables per stage; name what is excluded (surveys, specialist sub-consultants, BIM level, revisions count). Every unlisted "extra" is otherwise sole-sourced to the incumbent at their price.
- **Change-order control.** Require a priced variation rate card agreed up front, a written instruction before any chargeable work, and a cap on cumulative variations before re-approval. "Design development" must not be a billable variation for the firm's own coordination errors.
- **Professional indemnity (PI) insurance.** Specify a required level and that it is maintained *on an each-and-every-claim basis* and for a run-off period after completion — for latent design defects that surface years later. Do not name a figure as market-standard; set it to project risk and verify the certificate.
- **Liability caps.** Firms push to cap liability at a multiple of fee (or at the PI level). A cap set at the fee is often far below the loss a defective design can cause. Negotiate the cap against project value and carve out fraud, negligence, and IP infringement from any cap.
- **IP / design ownership + licence.** Default to you owning or holding an irrevocable licence to use, reproduce, and modify the design for the completed and future works. Firms retain copyright by default in many EU jurisdictions — secure at minimum a broad licence surviving termination.
- **Novation.** On design-and-build, the design team is often novated to the contractor. Insist on a pre-agreed novation deed and a duty-of-care/collateral warranty back to you, so you keep a direct claim.
- **Payment, retention, milestones.** Stage payments against accepted deliverables, not calendar. Hold retention (a withheld % released on completion/defects-liability expiry) as leverage for close-out.
