## Category-specific traps

## Category-specific traps

- "Percentage of cost is standard" — it is negotiable; the percentage basis rewards overspend.
- Front-loaded stage fees (heavy on early concept phases) so you've paid most of the fee before you can judge the design.
- Liability capped at the fee while PI cover is far higher — you're funding insurance you can't reach.
- Excluded sub-consultants (geotech, fire, façade) surfacing as costly variations.
- Copyright retained silently, blocking reuse or a follow-on firm.
- Vague "abortive work" and "additional services" clauses that convert coordination fixes into variations.

## What to trade (give low-value, get high-value)

## What to trade (give low-value, get high-value)

- **Give contract length / a framework of future projects (volume) → get a lump-sum rate cut and better IP/liability terms.** Pipeline certainty is real value to a practice.
- **Give faster payment days → get a fee reduction or a higher liability cap.** Professional firms are cash-sensitive.
- **Give a reasonable, project-scaled liability cap → get lower fee and full IP assignment.** Un-carved-out uncapped liability just buys a padded risk premium.
- **Give staged go/no-go commitment → get lower per-stage rates** in exchange for pipeline visibility.

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price** = the fee — its *basis* (%-of-cost / lump-sum / time-charge) and level. Prioritise basis and stage-gating over headline number.
- **payment_days** = when stage payments and retention release; a cash lever to trade for price or a higher liability cap.
- **contract_length** = single project vs multi-project framework; concede duration only for a rate cut plus IP/liability gains.
- **volume** = committed project pipeline; convert to framework pricing, not a one-off discount.
- **rebate** = retention and any performance/gainshare hold-back released on defect-free close-out.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Convert percentage-of-construction-cost fees to lump-sum on frozen scope; if a % stays, fix and cap the cost basis so scope growth can't auto-inflate the fee
- Gate fee to defined stages (RIBA Plan of Work / HOAI Leistungsphasen) with go/no-go and payment release at each phase
- Enumerate deliverables and explicit exclusions per stage; every unlisted item is otherwise a sole-sourced variation
- Agree a priced variation rate card, require written instruction before chargeable work, and cap cumulative variations before re-approval
- Specify PI insurance level on an each-and-every-claim basis with a run-off period for latent defects; verify the certificate
- Negotiate liability caps against project value not fee, and carve fraud/negligence/IP-infringement out of any cap
- Secure design IP ownership or an irrevocable licence surviving termination; don't let copyright be retained silently
- Pre-agree the novation deed and a collateral warranty / duty of care on design-and-build so you keep a direct claim

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

No figures are asserted as fact anywhere in this playbook — every one must be sourced live at negotiation time. Specifically: (1) HOAI stage-fee percentages and fee-table values — HOAI fees are advisory/freely negotiable since the 2019 ECJ ruling (C-377/17) and the 2021 HOAI reform; do NOT quote a table rate as a binding minimum, and confirm current legal status live. (2) Any "typical" or "market-standard" percentage-of-construction-cost fee — must come from live market benchmark or the buyer's own spend data. (3) Required PI insurance amounts and run-off period length — set to project risk from a live source / the buyer's insurance advisor, never a quoted default. (4) Liability cap multiples of fee — no standard number; derive from project value. (5) Retention percentage and defects-liability period length — contract/jurisdiction-specific, source per deal. (6) Variation rate-card day rates. (7) RIBA Plan of Work stage numbering (0–7) and HOAI Leistungsphasen (1–9) are named as real frameworks, but confirm the current published versions before citing specific stage content. A supplier-facing agent must not assert any of these as fact.

## What actually drives cost
Headline rent is the number everyone stares at, but the *net effective rent* — headline rent minus the value of all incentives, spread over the term to the first break — is what a tenant actually pays. A landlord will happily hold headline rent (it protects their asset valuation and the yield they report to investors) while giving away the equivalent value in rent-free months, fit-out cash, and service-charge concessions that never show in the headline. **Negotiate net effective, not headline.** Second cost driver: the *repairing and service-charge regime*, which quietly transfers the building's ageing and running costs onto the tenant. Third: *exit cost* — dilapidations and the absence of a break clause can turn a "cheap" lease into a trap.

## What actually drives cost
Headline rent is the number everyone stares at, but the *net effective rent* — headline rent minus the value of all incentives, spread over the term to the first break — is what a tenant actually pays. A landlord will happily hold headline rent (it protects their asset valuation and the yield they report to investors) while giving away the equivalent value in rent-free months, fit-out cash, and service-charge concessions that never show in the headline. **Negotiate net effective, not headline.** Second cost driver: the *repairing and service-charge regime*, which quietly transfers the building's ageing and running costs onto the tenant. Third: *exit cost* — dilapidations and the absence of a break clause can turn a "cheap" lease into a trap.

## The key levers (concrete)
- **Rent-free / incentive period.** The most flexible landlord give — it protects headline rent and thus valuation, so ask for it *first* and generously. Split it: part as pure rent-free (fit-out period), part as a contribution to cost of works.
- **Break clause.** A tenant-only, *unconditional* break is the single most valuable flexibility term. Fight every landlord condition — "vacant possession", "all rents paid", "material compliance" are the standard sabotage conditions that let a landlord defeat a break on a technicality. Demand: only pre-conditions are rent paid to date and vacant possession of the demise; no penalty for minor disrepair.
- **Rent review.** Prefer *indexed* (CPI/VPI-linked, ideally capped-and-collared) over *open-market* review. Note jurisdiction: "upward-only" reviews are a contractual feature predominantly seen in UK leases, not intrinsic to open-market review. In Germany, Staffelmiete (fixed step) and Indexmiete (index-linked) are two common escalation mechanisms — primarily developed in residential tenancy law (§557a/§557b BGB); commercial leases have wider freedom of contract, so confirm the applicable regime live. If open-market is unavoidable, strike "upward-only". In Germany a *Staffelmiete* (fixed step) or *Indexmiete* (index-linked) are the two statutory options — know which you're being offered.
- **Service charge.** Cap the annual increase (a % or index cap), exclude improvement/capex from the cap (you shouldn't fund the landlord upgrading their asset), and secure *audit rights* plus a cap on management fees. Get a "no-surprise" clause: charges not billed within a set window are waived.
- **Repairing obligations.** In UK/common-law leases, FRI (Full Repairing & Insuring) dumps the whole building's condition on the tenant while IRI (Internal Repairing) is far better — push FRI toward IRI. In a GERMAN commercial lease (Gewerbemiete, BGB) there is no FRI/IRI; repair allocation runs through Instandhaltung/Instandsetzung clauses and Schönheitsreparaturen case law (BGH) — negotiate which repairs the tenant bears there. Either way, if broad repairing liability is forced, bind it with a **schedule of condition** (photographic record at entry) so you hand it back as-received, not as-new.
- **Dilapidations.** Cap liability to the schedule of condition; exclude fair wear and tear; ideally convert to a fixed end-of-term payment agreed up front rather than an open-ended claim.
- **Fit-out contribution & security.** Trade a larger landlord fit-out contribution against holding headline rent. On security, push rent-deposit down and resist an over-long bank guarantee — every month of guarantee ties up your credit line.

## Category traps suppliers (landlords/agents) use
- **Upward-only rent review** dressed as "market standard". It isn't a law of nature — resist it.
- **Conditional break clauses** engineered to be un-exercisable.
- **Headline-rent anchoring** while conceding only invisible incentives — you feel like you won and still overpay net.
- **Uncapped service charges** with capex smuggled in.
- **FRI + no schedule of condition** = you inherit and hand back a fully-repaired building you never damaged.

## What to trade (give / get)
Give the landlord **headline rent and term certainty** (both protect their valuation and financing) to get **net-effective concessions and flexibility**: a longer commitment *to the first break* in exchange for a bigger rent-free, a service-charge cap, and a tenant-favourable dilapidations settlement. Give a modest security deposit to get FRI softened to IRI-with-schedule.

## Mapping to the engine's terms
- **price → net effective rent** (Direction: MINIMIZE). Score the incentive-adjusted figure, not headline.
- **contract_length → lease term to first break** (the flexibility trade lives here — longer term is a *concession you sell* for price/incentive gains).
- **payment_days → rent payment frequency/timing** (monthly vs quarterly-in-advance; German commercial leases often bill quarterly in advance — pushing toward monthly-in-arrears improves working capital).
- **rebate → rent-free period + fit-out contribution** (the landlord's cheapest give; weight it heavily as your primary "get").
- **volume → demised area / additional space or renewal options** (option to expand/contract, or take-up across multiple units, as a bundling lever).
Weight *price (net effective)* and *rebate (incentives)* highest for the buyer; hold *contract_length* as the low-personal-value term you concede to a term-hungry landlord. Never let the reservation floor drop below "unconditional tenant break + capped service charge".

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

Every quantitative figure in a live negotiation MUST be sourced, not asserted by the agent: (1) prevailing headline rent and market rent-free/incentive levels for the specific city, submarket, and asset class — pull from a live broker/valuation source (e.g. IPD/MSCI, JLL/CBRE/Colliers market reports), never quote a rent-free duration or €/m² as fact; (2) typical service-charge €/m² and management-fee percentages — market-specific, source live; (3) any statutory reference for Germany — the mechanics of Indexmiete vs Staffelmiete, permitted index (VPI/CPI), and notice/break rules under the BGB — must be confirmed against the current statute or legal counsel with a retrieval date, not stated from memory; capped-and-collared review percentages are deal-specific and unsourced here; (4) security deposit / bank-guarantee norms (number of months' rent) vary by covenant strength and market — source live; (5) the claim that open-market reviews are 'almost always upward-only' reflects a well-known market pattern but its prevalence in a given jurisdiction/period should be verified before asserting to a counterparty; (6) I did not verify current German statutory lease rules in this session — treat all legal-mechanism references as needing counsel/statute confirmation. The agent must present all such numbers as 'to be confirmed from [named source, dated]', never as established fact to the landlord.
## What drives cost

## What drives cost

Telecoms & AV cost is almost never the box price you're quoted — it's the **total cost of the estate over its life**: hardware, install/commissioning, cabling and infrastructure, the maintenance/support attach, and the refresh you're quietly locked into. Suppliers and their VARs/integrators earn thin margin on hardware and fat, recurring margin on **maintenance, support contracts, licences, and the next refresh**. The headline "discount off list" is theatre — list price is a fiction the vendor controls, so a big percentage off an inflated list can still be expensive. What actually moves total cost: **standardisation** (one platform, fewer SKUs, volume leverage), **interoperability** (open standards vs proprietary lock-in that dictates who supplies the next phase), **the labour lines** (install, commissioning, cabling — often sole-sourced to the integrator), and the **support/SLA tier** you're steered into. In an EU/German context, framework agreements and public tenders (EU procurement / CPV-coded) shape leverage, and standards conformance (e.g. open AV-over-IP, SIP for telephony) is your best anti-lock-in weapon.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Price to net, not to discount-off-list.** Demand the net unit price and benchmark it; a percentage off a list the vendor sets is meaningless. Decompose every quote: hardware, install/commissioning, cabling/infrastructure, maintenance, warranty, spares — negotiate each line, never an all-in bundle you can't unpick.
- **Unbundle install/commissioning.** Bundled install hides labour margin and ties you to one integrator. Get it separately priced and, where possible, competed — or use it as a concession you *grant* for a hardware/maintenance cut, not a default.
- **Attack the maintenance/support attach.** This is the recurring profit centre. Push the SLA tier down to what you actually need, cap annual uplift, define response/fix times with teeth, and refuse maintenance that silently renews. Question whether year-1 warranty makes paid support redundant.
- **Standardise to build volume leverage.** Consolidating on fewer platforms/SKUs turns scattered spend into committed volume — your strongest price lever. Grant standardisation only in exchange for price/rebate.
- **Break interoperability lock-in.** Insist on open standards and documented interfaces so the *next* phase isn't sole-sourced to the incumbent. Proprietary codecs, closed control systems, and vendor-only cabling are lock-in traps priced as "integration."
- **Warranty, spares, RMA as real terms.** Extend warranty coverage, negotiate advance-replacement RMA and defined turnaround, and secure guaranteed spares availability / end-of-life notice so you're not forced into an early refresh.
- **Frame capex vs opex deliberately.** Outright purchase (capex) vs lease/DaaS/managed (opex) changes cash profile and who owns refresh risk. Opex models often bury a high effective interest rate and a refresh lock-in — make the financing cost explicit before choosing.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- **Inflated list price → headline discount** that still leaves you above market net.
- **Loss-leader hardware, recovered on maintenance/support** and the mandatory refresh.
- **Proprietary lock-in sold as "integration"** so phase 2 and the refresh are sole-sourced to them.
- **Bundled install/cabling** hiding labour margin and blocking competitive tender of the work.
- **Auto-renewing, uplift-uncapped maintenance** and SLAs with no real remedy for breach.
- **Early end-of-life / end-of-support** engineered to force a premature (full-price) refresh.
- **Opex/DaaS quotes** that hide the financing rate and lock you into the vendor's refresh cycle.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give contract length → get price + capped maintenance.** A longer term lets the vendor amortise install and plan; extract a real net-price cut, a maintenance uplift cap, and a mid-term benchmarking/break right. Never give length without the exit and price protection.
- **Give committed volume / standardisation → get rebate + lower net.** Consolidating SKUs is high value to the vendor and something you'd do anyway — convert it into tiered pricing or a retro rebate, not just a one-off discount.
- **Give faster payment days → get price.** Improved cash flow has genuine value to a hardware-heavy supplier and their distributor; a low-weight give for you, trade it for net price or warranty extension.
- **Give bundled install (as a granted concession) → get hardware/maintenance cut** — only when unbundling can't be competed and you extract value for it.
- **Take rebate over headline discount** when the vendor resists a net-price cut — a volume-triggered rebate returns the same money and only pays if volume lands.

## Engine mapping

## Engine mapping

- **price** = the *net* unit price plus the maintenance/support line and install — prioritise net and recurring cost over discount-off-list.
- **payment_days** = DPO; a low-weight give to trade for price or warranty.
- **contract_length** = the amortisation/lock-in lever — concede only with a benchmarking/break right and capped maintenance uplift.
- **volume** = standardised/committed estate — convert to rebate and lower net, not just discount.
- **rebate** = the retro mechanism where volume commitment and standardisation are returned as cash.

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook contains NO benchmark numbers, and none may be invented at negotiation time. Every figure a buyer would want must be sourced live and cited with a retrieval date; a supplier-facing agent must state these as "to be benchmarked", never assert them as fact:
- Net market price / typical discount-off-list for the specific hardware (switchboards/PABX, handsets, AV displays, codecs, cabling, routers) — benchmark against live distributor pricing, recent bids, or the buyer's own spend data; the list price itself is vendor-set and must not be treated as a market anchor.
- Maintenance/support attach rate as a % of hardware, typical SLA response/fix times per tier, and standard annual uplift/indexation caps — source from the actual quotes and a live market benchmark.
- Install/commissioning and cabling labour rates (per point/per day/per m) — get current market/integrator rates for the region.
- Warranty durations, RMA/advance-replacement turnaround norms, and spares/end-of-life notice periods — confirm from the vendor's published terms and market norm at negotiation time.
- The effective financing/interest rate embedded in any lease/DaaS/opex quote — require the vendor to disclose it; do not estimate it.
- Volume-rebate thresholds and tier percentages — set from the buyer's real committed volume, not a made-up figure.
- EU/German procurement specifics (framework-agreement mechanics, EU procurement thresholds, CPV coding, applicable standards conformance) are real but must be confirmed against the current statutory/tender source for the specific contract, with date, rather than asserted from memory.
## What drives cost

## What drives cost

Telecom cost is **unit rate × consumed quantity + surcharge/overage creep + lifecycle lock-in** — and most of the leak is below the headline per-line price. The carrier concedes on the advertised per-line/per-GB rate and recovers it through **overage rates, out-of-bundle usage, roaming, premium/service numbers, and tariff sprawl** (dozens of legacy plans, each with its own rate, so nobody can benchmark). Cost also swings with **pooling** (a shared data pool across all lines almost always beats per-line allowances — heavy users borrow from light ones instead of every line paying overage), **technology-refresh timing** (a long fixed term locks you to today's tech and hardware while the market keeps cutting the effective rate), and **connectivity architecture** (buying capacity/circuits vs. metered usage). For postal/courier: **volume banding, weight/format steps, and surcharges** (fuel, remote-area, peak, residential, dimensional weight) do the same job accessorials do in freight. Award is typically a **framework/tender across a service matrix** (mobile, fixed data, voice, IoT/M2M SIMs, postal tiers) — you allocate a portfolio, not one number.

Award is typically a **framework/tender across a service matrix** (mobile, fixed data, voice, IoT/M2M SIMs, postal tiers) — you allocate a portfolio, not one number.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Consolidate and simplify the tariff first.** Collapse legacy plans into a small standard set before you price. Sprawl is the carrier's shield against benchmarking; a clean catalogue is your precondition for competition.
- **Pool data, don't allowance it per line.** Negotiate one shared pool with an aggregate overage rate, and cap or zero-rate overage inside the pool. Per-line allowances manufacture overage revenue.
- **Attack overage and out-of-bundle rates as their own line.** Cap the per-GB overage rate, bundle or flatten premium/service-number and international-direct-dial, and define what "unlimited" excludes (fair-use throttling).
- **Roaming: get EU regulated + a defined rest-of-world tariff.** Inside the EU, roam-like-at-home is the statutory baseline (verify current rules live); the negotiable prize is a capped or bundled **rest-of-world** roaming tariff and a hard bill-shock cap.
- **Decouple term from technology-refresh risk.** If you give a longer term, extract a mid-term tech-refresh/price-review right (benchmark-down clause) and a device-refresh path so the term doesn't freeze you at today's rate.
- **Keep MVNO / aggregator competition credible.** Carriers price against the threat that you move to an MVNO or a managed-mobility aggregator. Name it. It disciplines the incumbent's "we run the network" premium.
- **Buy SLA credits that bite.** Availability targets for circuits/connectivity with **service credits that are automatic and material** — not a token gesture you have to claim through a dispute process.
- **Protect exit: portability + early-termination.** Number portability guaranteed and fast, no clawback of past discounts on early exit, and staged (not cliff) termination fees. This is your BATNA insurance.
- **Postal/courier: band the volume, cap the surcharges.** Negotiate volume tiers, weight/format step boundaries, and fix/cap fuel and ancillary surcharges the same way you'd cap an FSC.

## Category-specific traps

## Category-specific traps

- **Tariff sprawl** — dozens of legacy plans kept alive precisely so you can't benchmark or pool.
- **"Unlimited" with silent throttling / fair-use** that reintroduces overage economics.
- **Overage and premium-number rates left uncapped** while the per-line rate is cut to win the deal.
- **Auto-renewal / evergreen rollover** and discount clawback on early termination that trap you past the tech-refresh point.
- **Bundled "free" handsets** amortised into the line rate at a hidden financing markup — separate device cost from airtime.
- **Roaming and rest-of-world** left to standard tariff, and **bill-shock** with no spend cap.
- **Postal surcharges** (fuel, remote-area, dimensional weight, peak) undefined or "per published tariff" so they ratchet mid-term.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give contract length, get unit rate — but only with a tech-refresh/benchmark-down right.** A longer term is cheap to you *if* it can't freeze the rate; trade it for a lower per-line/per-GB rate plus a mid-term price review.
- **Give volume / line-count consolidation, get rate + a rebate tier.** Committing your whole estate (and IoT/M2M SIMs) to one carrier is high value to them; route it for a lower pooled rate and a volume-triggered rebate.
- **Give payment days, get price.** Faster payment is real cash-flow value; concede it (a low-weight term for most buyers) to pull down the overage cap or the rest-of-world roaming tariff — a clean logroll.
- **Take a rebate over a headline cut** when the carrier defends list price: a volume/spend-triggered rebate returns the same money, protects their reference price, and only pays if volume lands.
- **Hold the line on overage caps, SLA credits, roaming, and exit terms** — these are your high-weight terms; don't spend concessions there.

## Engine mapping

## Engine mapping

- **price** = per-line + per-GB + capped overage + roaming + postal band/surcharge rates (minimize); decompose it, never one all-in number.
- **contract length** = currency you concede for rate — but pair every extension with a tech-refresh/benchmark-down clause.
- **volume** = estate/line-count/SIM consolidation and postal volume bands, routed to win rate and a rebate tier.
- **payment days** = low-weight give, high supplier value; spend it to pull down overage/roaming.
- **rebate** = the face-saving substitute for a list-price cut, tied to committed volume/spend.

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook contains NO specific numbers, and every figure below must be sourced live before an agent asserts it — a supplier-facing agent must never state any of these as fact from memory: (1) EU roaming rules — "roam-like-at-home" and any regulated wholesale/retail caps or fair-use limits must be confirmed against the current EU Roaming Regulation in force at negotiation time, not assumed; (2) any per-line, per-GB, or overage benchmark rate must be benchmarked against live current market bids/indices for the region and volume; (3) typical/target overage caps, SLA availability percentages, and service-credit amounts must be pulled from current market norms and the carrier's published SLA, not invented; (4) rest-of-world roaming rates, premium/service-number rates, and bill-shock cap levels must be sourced from live tariffs; (5) postal/courier volume-band boundaries, weight/format step rates, and surcharge levels (fuel, remote-area, dimensional weight, peak) must be taken from the carrier's current published tariff and a live rate benchmark; (6) any MVNO/aggregator price delta vs. the incumbent, any rebate threshold or tier percentage, and any early-termination fee schedule must be sourced from actual current quotes. State all of these as "to be benchmarked" with the live source and retrieval date. Also unverified here and to confirm live if in scope: whether German/EU public-procurement thresholds or framework rules (e.g. VgV/GWB) constrain the award mechanism for this specific buyer.

## What drives cost in this category

Training cost is a stack, not a day rate. The pools:

1. **Delivery labour** — instructor-led day rate (per trainer per day) or e-learning platform PEPM (per-enrolled-user-per-month / per-seat licence). This is the visible line and where suppliers steer the haggle.
2. **Content development** — bespoke course build, often quoted once but the vendor keeps the IP and re-sells the same content to your competitors. You paid to build it and cannot reuse it.
3. **Pricing-unit arbitrage** — per-seat, per-cohort, and licence models price the *same* delivery very differently. A per-seat quote punishes you for filling the room; per-cohort rewards it. Vendors quote the unit that maximises their take against your expected fill.
4. **Cancellation / rescheduling and materials** — late-cancel and reschedule fees, printed/licensed materials, LMS hosting, and travel/expenses stacked on the instructor rate.

The defining trap: the supplier makes you negotiate the headline day rate or PEPM while protecting content ownership, the pricing unit, and cancellation terms — where the real money and lock-in live.

## The key levers (concrete)

## The key levers (concrete)

- **Force the right pricing unit.** For high-fill internal cohorts, buy **per-cohort/per-day** (fixed cost, you keep the seat-fill upside) not per-seat. For sparse, long-tail audiences, per-seat or PEPM shifts risk to the vendor. Make them quote all three and pick the one that fits *your* utilisation — never accept the unit they lead with.
- **Attack PEPM on the active-user definition.** E-learning PEPM is billed on *enrolled* or *provisioned* seats, not *active* learners. Negotiate billing on active/consumed seats, a true-down (not just true-up) clause, and no charge for dormant licences. This is often the single biggest e-learning saving.
- **Content ownership / reuse rights.** Decide up front: work-for-hire (you own the bespoke content you funded), a perpetual internal-reuse licence, or a discount because they retain resale rights. Never pay full bespoke-build price *and* leave IP with the vendor — that is paying twice.
- **Train-the-trainer to internalise.** Buy the right to certify your own trainers so recurring delivery moves in-house. This caps your dependence and is your strongest long-run price lever — pay a one-off certification fee to escape a perpetual day-rate.
- **Cancellation / rescheduling terms.** Push the fee cliff back (e.g. free reschedule outside a defined window) and make cancellation a sliding scale, not a flat penalty. Vendors default to aggressive cliffs because unfilled trainer days are their risk — make them share it.
- **Outcome / completion-based pricing.** Where measurable (certification pass rate, completion %), tie a slice of fee to outcomes to claw back spend on no-shows and non-completers. Needs a clean, pre-agreed KPI or it becomes a dispute.
- **Materials.** Unbundle printed/licensed materials from the day rate; prefer digital/reusable, and get per-learner material cost itemised so it is not a hidden per-head tax.
- **Volume commitment vs flexibility.** Committed seats/cohorts unlock the best unit price but strand you if demand drops. Trade a realistic floor commitment for the rate, and keep a flex band above it priced at the same unit — don't over-commit to a forecast.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- **Per-seat quote on a room you'll fill anyway** — you pay for utilisation you were always going to deliver.
- **PEPM on provisioned, not active, seats** — you fund licences nobody logs into.
- **Bespoke build billed to you, IP retained by vendor** — you paid R&D; they resell it.
- **Aggressive cancellation cliffs** framed as "standard terms" — they are highly negotiable.
- **"Included" materials or LMS hosting** that quietly inflate the per-head or PEPM line.
- **Content refresh / update fees** that turn a one-time build into an annuity.

## What to trade (give low-value to get high-value)

## What to trade (give low-value to get high-value)

- **Give longer contract length + committed volume → get a lower unit rate, a broad internal-reuse licence, and train-the-trainer rights.** Vendors weight utilisation certainty highly; you weight unit price and lock-in escape higher.
- **Give faster payment (shorter payment days) → get a rate or PEPM concession.** Cash flow is a common supplier ask and cheap for you to concede if cash allows.
- **Give a realistic floor volume commitment → get a bigger volume rebate and softer cancellation terms.**
- **Hold the line on content ownership, active-user billing, and audit of completion data** — near-zero cost to you, and they attack the hidden cost and lock-in directly. Never trade them for a headline day-rate discount.

## Mapping to the engine terms

## Mapping to the engine terms

- **price** (MINIMIZE) → the day rate, per-cohort fee, per-seat price, or PEPM — attack the *unit* and its definition, not just the headline number.
- **payment_days** (MAXIMIZE for buyer) → billing terms; a cheap give for you, a strong get for the vendor. Model as a give-term with high supplier appetite.
- **contract_length** → tenure; trade length for unit-price cuts, reuse rights, and train-the-trainer.
- **volume** → committed seats/cohorts/spend that unlocks the best unit price and rebate bands.
- **rebate_pct** (MAXIMIZE) → retrospective volume rebate on committed training spend; a get-term won via term + volume.
- Content ownership/reuse rights, train-the-trainer certification, cancellation-schedule softness, and outcome-based fee-at-risk are category terms outside the core five — carry them as explicit logrolling give/get terms (typically high buyer weight on ownership and active-user billing).

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Make the vendor quote per-seat, per-cohort, and licence/PEPM, then pick the unit that fits your actual fill — never accept the one they lead with
- Bill e-learning PEPM on active/consumed seats with a true-down clause, not on provisioned or enrolled licences
- Settle content ownership up front: work-for-hire or a perpetual internal-reuse licence, never full bespoke-build price with IP left at the vendor
- Buy train-the-trainer certification to internalise recurring delivery and escape a perpetual day rate
- Turn cancellation/reschedule cliffs into a sliding scale with a free-reschedule window
- Tie a fee slice to completion/certification outcomes where a clean KPI exists, to claw back no-show and non-completion spend
- Unbundle and itemise materials and LMS hosting so they aren't a hidden per-head tax
- Trade contract length + a realistic floor volume for a lower unit rate, reuse rights, a bigger rebate, and softer cancellation terms; concede payment days for a rate cut

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook asserts NO specific numbers, and the agent must not either. Every figure a real negotiation needs must be sourced live and cited, never fabricated in a supplier-facing message: (1) prevailing instructor-led day-rate and PEPM/per-seat benchmarks by topic, seniority, language, and region — pull from a current rate benchmark, do not assume; (2) typical cancellation/reschedule fee windows and percentages — these are vendor- and contract-specific, quote from the actual terms sheet not from memory; (3) standard content-development cost and any refresh/update fee levels — get the vendor's itemised build cost, do not estimate; (4) train-the-trainer certification fee and any per-trainer recurring licence; (5) payment-term norms for the client's region/sector; (6) volume-rebate band thresholds. Legal/tax points to verify before asserting, not assume: German/EU VAT treatment of training services (some vocational/further-education training can be VAT-exempt under UStG §4 / the EU VAT Directive — this is fact-specific and must be confirmed with tax/legal, never stated as settled); whether public funding or co-financing rules apply (e.g. Bildungsgutschein / AZAV-certified provision, ESF-funded programmes) which can constrain pricing and eligibility; and the enforceability of IP/work-for-hire and completion-data audit clauses under the governing law. Present all of these as "benchmark/verify this live," never as a fixed figure.
## What drives cost

## What drives cost

This category is really four sub-markets bundled under one owner, and the cost lever differs in each. **Air** cost is driven by route/cabin mix and share-of-wallet, not a single "discount." **Hotel** cost is the negotiated corporate rate versus the open BAR (best available rate), plus whether last-room-availability (LRA) and inclusions (breakfast, wifi, parking) actually hold. **The TMC** (travel management company) is not where your spend sits — it's an intermediary whose fee model quietly decides whether it steers travellers toward *your* savings or *its* supplier incentives. **Catering / meetings & events (MICE)** cost is cost-per-head (or cost-per-cover) versus subsidy, plus minimum-spend and attrition/cancellation clauses. The biggest structural driver across all four is **leakage and compliance**: negotiated rates are worthless if travellers book off-programme, so adoption/channel-share is the true price lever. Money also hides in **soft-dollar incentives** (airline/hotel signing bonuses, free tickets, marketing funds) that flow to the buyer *organisation* but can distort category choices if not governed.

Money also hides in **soft-dollar incentives** (airline/hotel signing bonuses, free tickets, marketing funds) that flow to the buyer *organisation* but can distort category choices if not governed.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Hotel: negotiate LRA and dynamic-vs-fixed deliberately.** A fixed corporate rate is only real if it's LRA (available whenever a room is) — a non-LRA rate that vanishes on busy nights is a trap. A dynamic rate (a fixed % off BAR) protects you in soft markets but exposes you in peaks; a fixed rate does the reverse. Choose per property by demand pattern, and always pin the *inclusions*.
- **TMC: separate the fee model from the savings.** Push for transparent transaction-fee (per-booking) pricing or a management fee, and demand full disclosure of supplier rebates/overrides the TMC earns. A "free"/rebate-funded TMC is paid by your suppliers to route your travellers — a structural conflict. Insist on rebate transparency and, ideally, that overrides are passed back or netted.
- **Air: trade share-of-wallet for a corporate discount and soft-dollar terms.** Airline deals reward committed share on specific routes. Negotiate the point-of-sale/route-specific discount, waivers & favours, and lounge/status perks — and govern soft-dollar incentives as programme value, not a personal perk.
- **Catering: cost-per-head with a floor, not open subsidy.** Move from an open subsidy (you top up whatever it costs) to a capped cost-per-head with defined menu tiers. Attack food-cost pass-through, labour, and management fee separately as in facilities.
- **Meetings & events: unbundle, then re-bundle as a concession.** M&E and transient hotel spend are separate negotiations; bundle them only to *earn* a rate cut, and control attrition, cancellation, and minimum-spend clauses — these are where hotels recover margin.
- **Volume commitment / minimum spend as currency, not a gift.** Commit measurable share you'd spend anyway; keep any minimum-spend or attrition floor soft (a band, not a penalty cliff).
- **Rebate / volume tiers over headline discount.** When a supplier resists a rate cut, a spend-triggered rebate returns the same money and only pays if the volume lands.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- **Non-LRA "corporate rates"** quoted as a discount but unavailable exactly when you travel.
- **BAR-relative dynamic rates with no cap**, so the "discount" floats on a rate the hotel controls.
- **Rebate-funded / "free" TMC** whose supplier overrides steer travellers away from your best fares.
- **Undisclosed supplier overrides and GDS incentives** the TMC keeps.
- **Attrition & cancellation clauses** on M&E that bill you for unfilled room blocks / covers.
- **Minimum-spend cliffs** and **soft-dollar incentives** dangled to lock in share regardless of actual best value.
- **Inclusions stripped after signing** (breakfast/wifi/parking billed back).

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give share-of-wallet / committed volume → get air discount + hotel rate + rebate tier.** Route committed share to the supplier's priority routes/properties — high value to them, spend you'd make anyway.
- **Give contract length → get a firmer rate and LRA guarantee.** Longer term lets hotels/airlines plan; extract the rate cut *and* a benchmarking/market-review right so you're not locked above market.
- **Give faster payment days → get price or rebate.** Fewer payment days is genuine cash-flow value to hotels and caterers; a clean low-weight give to pull down cost.
- **Give M&E/meeting bundling → get transient rate cut and fee reduction.** Grant the bundle only for a concession.
- **Take a rebate when a supplier won't cut the headline rate** — verifiable, contingent on realised volume.

## Engine mapping

## Engine mapping

- **price** = negotiated hotel rate (fixed or % off BAR), TMC transaction fee, air discount, catering cost-per-head/subsidy cap. Minimize; prioritise fee transparency and LRA over a headline number.
- **payment_days** = DPO; low-weight give, high supplier value — trade for price/rebate.
- **contract_length** = amortisation lever; concede only bundled with a benchmarking/market-review right.
- **volume** = committed share-of-wallet / room nights / covers / minimum spend; keep floors soft.
- **rebate** = spend/volume-triggered return and the vehicle for netting TMC overrides back to you.

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

This playbook contains NO invented figures, and a supplier-facing agent must not assert any of the following as fact — each must be sourced live at negotiation time and cited with a retrieval date: (1) any hotel corporate-rate discount %, the current BAR for a property, or a typical % off BAR — pull from live RFP/rate data (e.g. an RFP tool) for the specific property/date. (2) TMC transaction-fee levels, management-fee amounts, and the size of supplier overrides/GDS incentives — these vary by market and contract and must come from the actual TMC bid and disclosure, not an assumed 'brokers take X%'. (3) Airline corporate-discount percentages, route-specific fares, and soft-dollar/waiver values — from the airline's actual proposal and your own booking data. (4) Catering cost-per-head, food-cost %, and prevailing subsidy levels — from the specific caterer's bid and a live market benchmark. (5) Attrition/cancellation percentages, minimum-spend thresholds, and volume-rebate tier breakpoints — contract-specific, to be negotiated and sourced, never defaulted. (6) VAT treatment of travel, hotel, catering and MICE spend, and any EU/German statutory rules on entertainment/hospitality deductibility — must be confirmed against current tax guidance for the jurisdiction, not stated from memory. (7) Adoption/channel-share and leakage rates — must come from the buyer's own travel-programme data. Any share-of-wallet or committed-volume figure used as a lever must be the buyer's real spend, not an estimate.

## What drives cost

Installation cost is **labour hours × loaded day-rate + materials/consumables + risk premium + subcontractor markup + preliminaries (prelims: access, welfare, plant, supervision)**. The single biggest cost driver is not the headline rate — it is **who owns the unknowns**. Suppliers price site-condition uncertainty, access risk, and scope gaps as fat contingency baked into the number. Whoever carries that risk pays for it. The second driver is **productivity assumptions**: hours per unit installed, assumed clear access, assumed working hours. A supplier who assumes obstructed access and out-of-hours work quietly inflates the base.

## The key levers

## The key levers

- **Fixed-price (lump sum) vs day-rate.** Push for fixed-price against a *defined* scope and drawings — it forces the supplier to own their productivity and contingency. Accept day-rate only for genuinely undefinable work, and cap it (rate card + a not-to-exceed ceiling + your right to instruct a stop). Suppliers prefer day-rate precisely because it transfers scope risk back to you.
- **Site-condition / scope-risk allocation.** The classic trap is a low base price plus wide-open "unforeseen conditions" and "unless otherwise stated" carve-outs that become variations later. Demand a **site survey before pricing**, a documented list of assumptions/exclusions, and that the supplier is deemed to have satisfied themselves on access and conditions. Convert as many named risks as possible into the fixed price.
- **Snagging / defect-liability period (DLP).** In EU/DE the statutory warranty (Gewährleistung) for building/installation work under the German BGB / VOB/B is a real floor you should not negotiate below — the exact period must be sourced live, not asserted here. Push the *contractual* snagging and make-good obligations, response times for defects, and that final acceptance (Abnahme) starts the clock, not practical completion.
- **Retention.** Withhold a retention percentage released in two tranches (at acceptance and at end of DLP) as security for snagging — real leverage. The retention %/split are a live-sourced/market-driven negotiation point.
- **Out-of-hours / premium labour.** Insist premium rates apply only to hours *you instruct* out-of-hours, at a pre-agreed uplift, not as a default on the whole job. Reject blanket "shift premium" loaded into the base.
- **Phasing & access constraints.** Access and phasing are where suppliers claim disruption/standing-time. Pin down access windows, sequencing, and who bears standing-time in the contract. Give realistic access to buy a lower price; vague access invites padded contingency.
- **Make-good & reinstatement.** Define make-good scope explicitly (cores, penetrations, finishes) so it is in the price — otherwise it returns as a variation.
- **H&S / compliance cost.** RAMS, CDM-equivalent duties, permits, and site-safety are the supplier's obligation and should be in the base, not a line-item surcharge. Confirm compliance is priced in, not bolted on.
- **Subcontractor markup.** Ask for the markup on subcontracted packages to be disclosed and capped; require open-book on subbed work above a threshold.
- **Tie to equipment supply vs standalone.** Bundling install with the equipment supplier can cut interface risk and give a single throat to choke — but kills price tension. Keep install **competitively tenderable** even if you buy the kit from one vendor, or use the equipment volume as leverage on the install rate.

## Category-specific traps

## Category-specific traps

- "Provisional sums" and "to be confirmed on site" — a blank cheque; force fixed pricing or a capped allowance.
- Variation/change-order margins far above base margin — agree the variation markup up front.
- Retention released too early, or acceptance signed before snagging closes.
- Prelims and standing-time recharged for access delays you didn't cause.

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give payment days, get price.** Faster payment (fewer PAYMENT_DAYS) is cheap for you if you're liquid and materially eases a contractor's cash flow — trade it for a lower PRICE or tighter DLP.
- **Give volume/pipeline, get rate.** Committing a programme of sites (VOLUME_UNITS) or a longer framework (CONTRACT_MONTHS) justifies a lower installed rate and standing crews.
- **Give access certainty, get contingency back.** Firm access windows and clean drawings let the supplier drop padded risk from PRICE.
- **Get, don't give, on retention and DLP** — these are your defect security; concede price elsewhere before weakening them.

## Mapping to the engine terms

## Mapping to the engine terms

- **price** → installed lump sum or day-rate (the risk-loaded number; your primary MINIMIZE term).
- **payment_days** → your cheapest concession currency; trade down against price.
- **contract_months** → framework length; longer term ↔ lower rate, standing crews, priority.
- **volume_units** → number of sites/units/installs committed; the discount justifier.
- **rebate_pct** → volume rebate or retention-style holdback proxy; ties realized spend to price relief.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Fixed-price against defined drawings — make the supplier own productivity and contingency; cap and ceiling any day-rate
- Force a pre-price site survey and a written assumptions/exclusions list so unforeseen-conditions can't become variations
- Hold retention released in tranches (acceptance + end of defect-liability period) as snagging security
- Restrict premium/out-of-hours rates to hours YOU instruct at a pre-agreed uplift — reject shift premium in the base
- Pin access windows, phasing, and standing-time liability in the contract; trade firm access for dropped contingency
- Put make-good, RAMS/H&S, permits, and reinstatement inside the base price, not as surcharges
- Disclose and cap subcontractor markup; open-book on subbed packages above a threshold
- Agree the variation/change-order markup up front and ban open 'provisional sums' — cap or fix any allowance

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

Nothing quantitative was asserted as fact. Every figure a buyer or a supplier-facing agent would need MUST be sourced live, not invented: (1) the German/EU statutory warranty/Gewährleistung period for construction and installation work under the BGB and VOB/B (differs by regime and work type) — do not state a year count without a legal source and retrieval date; (2) typical/standard retention percentage and its release split; (3) benchmark installed day-rates, loaded labour rates, and hours-per-unit productivity for the specific trade and region; (4) market subcontractor markup percentages; (5) out-of-hours/shift premium uplift percentages; (6) typical variation/change-order margins; (7) defect-liability-period lengths that are contractual rather than statutory. The distinction between statutory minimums (Gewährleistung / VOB/B — a floor set by law) and negotiable contractual terms is real but the specific durations and rates must be confirmed against current statute and live market data before any number reaches a counterparty.

## What drives cost

These services look like commodities but their cost is built from a **unit rate × volume × premium tiers** stack, plus recurring liabilities suppliers hope you never scrutinise. The margin hides in three places: (1) **rush/turnaround premiums** billed as if every job were urgent; (2) **records storage rent** — a monthly per-box/per-GB fee that compounds for the life of the archive, long after the digitisation project ends; and (3) **reuse suppliers don't credit back to you** — translation memory (TM) and print plates/setup you already paid to create. Volume is your strongest lever because these are high-fixed-cost operations: the supplier wants throughput and predictability, and will trade rate for committed volume and a longer framework.

## The key levers (concrete)

## The key levers (concrete)

- **Decompose the unit rate.** Never accept a blended per-word/per-page/per-unit price. Split into base rate, urgency surcharge, project-management/handling fee, and minimum-charge. Suppliers pad the minimum charge and the PM fee because they're less visible than headline rate.
- **Translation: demand TM leverage.** Insist on tiered rates by match category (new / fuzzy / 100%-match / repetitions). You own the TM — write ownership and portability into the contract so you can leave. Refuse to pay full rate on content you've already translated.
- **Print/records: unbundle setup from run, and storage from retrieval.** Setup/plates amortise over the run; storage rent is separate from retrieval and from destruction. Get the **destruction/exit cost quoted up front** — that's where you get held hostage at end-of-term.
- **Turnaround tiers, not a flat rush.** Define standard/expedited/emergency SLAs with distinct prices, and a rule that only buyer-flagged jobs incur rush. Cap the share of spend that can carry a surcharge.
- **Volume banding with real minimums.** Band the rate down as annual volume rises, but scrutinise any minimum-volume commitment — an unmet minimum is a penalty you pay for demand you didn't have.
- **Framework over spot** for predictable flow (locks rate, kills per-job haggling); keep spot for spiky, one-off work so you're not paying framework overhead on it.
- **Quality/error SLA with teeth:** defined error rate, free correction/re-do, and a service-credit/rebate mechanism — not just an apology.

- **Translation: demand TM leverage.** Insist on tiered rates by match category (new / fuzzy / 100%-match / repetitions). You own the TM — write ownership and portability into the contract so you can leave. Refuse to pay full rate on content you've already translated.
- **Print/records: unbundle setup from run, and storage from retrieval.** Setup/plates amortise over the run; storage rent is separate from retrieval and from destruction. Get the **destruction/exit cost quoted up front** — that's where you get held hostage at end-of-term.

- **Quality/error SLA with teeth:** defined error rate, free correction/re-do, and a service-credit/rebate mechanism — not just an apology.

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

## Category-specific traps suppliers use

- **Minimum charge inflation** — a high floor per job turns cheap small jobs into expensive ones.
- **Rush-by-default** — everything quoted at expedited tier unless you police it.
- **TM lock-in** — supplier holds the memory, so leaving means re-paying for translations you funded.
- **Storage that outlives value** — you pay rent on boxes/scans no one will ever retrieve; digitise-then-destroy is cheaper but they profit from perpetual storage.
- **Exit/destruction fees** buried in the small print, priced at end-of-term when you have no leverage.
- **Word/page count method** — counting source vs target, or a padded page definition, quietly moves the bill.

## What to trade (give / get)

## What to trade (give / get)

- **Give payment days, get rate.** Faster payment (shorter payment days) is cheap for you if cash allows and genuinely valuable to these often mid-size suppliers — trade it for unit-rate reduction.
- **Give contract length + volume commitment, get banded rates, TM/reuse discounts, and locked rush caps.** A longer framework with committed volume is your biggest chip.
- **Give predictable, batched work (fewer rush jobs), get a lower standard rate.** Rush avoidance is real cost you can hand over.
- **Take rate as rebate when they won't cut list price:** a volume-triggered retrospective rebate preserves their headline rate while returning cash to you.

## Mapping to the engine's terms

## Mapping to the engine's terms

- **price** = the unit rate stack (per-word/page/unit, plus surcharge and minimum-charge — model the effective blended rate).
- **payment days** = your cheapest give; concede here to move price.
- **contract length** = framework term; longer term justifies deeper banding and TM/reuse discounts.
- **volume** = committed annual volume driving the band; also the trigger for rebate.
- **rebate** = retrospective volume rebate and SLA service-credits — the mechanism to recover value when list price won't move.

Logroll: concede **payment days** and **contract length** (low buyer weight if cash and demand are stable) to win **price** and **rebate** (high weight). Never trade away the **error SLA** or **TM ownership** — those are floor terms, not chips.

## Key levers

## Key levers

- Decompose the unit rate into base, urgency surcharge, PM/handling fee, and minimum charge — refuse blended pricing
- Translation: tier rates by TM match category (new/fuzzy/100%/repetitions) and secure TM ownership + portability
- Unbundle print setup/plates from run cost, and records storage rent from retrieval and destruction
- Get the exit/destruction cost quoted up front, before you're locked in at end-of-term
- Define standard/expedited/emergency turnaround tiers with distinct prices and cap the surcharge-eligible spend share
- Band unit rate down by committed annual volume, but stress-test any minimum-volume penalty clause
- Framework for predictable flow (locks rate), spot for spiky one-offs (avoids framework overhead)
- Attach a quality/error SLA with free re-do plus service-credit/rebate, not just remediation

## Sourcing discipline

## Sourcing discipline

I asserted NO specific numbers, market rates, or percentages, per the honesty rule. Every figure a buyer agent will eventually need MUST be sourced live before use in supplier-facing prose, specifically: (1) prevailing per-word/per-page/per-unit rates for the language pair, print run, or service — vary by market and must come from live quotes or a rate benchmark, not memory; (2) typical TM match discount ladders (fuzzy/100%/repetition percentages) — real CAT-tool grid conventions exist but the exact discount tiers are commercially negotiated and must be sourced from the actual supplier's grid; (3) records-storage per-box/per-GB monthly rent and destruction/retrieval fees — quote-specific; (4) any rush/expedited surcharge magnitude; (5) statutory context — EU Late Payment Directive (2011/7/EU, recast pending) sets default payment-term limits and interest for B2B/public bodies, and German public procurement (GWB/VgV, UVgO for below-threshold) governs framework use — the exact current limits, thresholds, and interest rates must be verified against the live statute/directive with a retrieval date, not stated from memory; (6) any claim about what suppliers or brokers "typically" charge as margin — must be sourced, never invented. A supplier-facing agent must present none of these as fact without a live source and retrieval date.
